title: Context-Aware Two-Stage Training for Domain Generalization in Speech Separation
authors: Wupeng Wang, Zexu Pan, Jingru Lin, Shuai Wang, Haizhou Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Speech separation aims to isolate individual speech signals from multi-talker speech mixtures. Despite the impressive results on synthetic benchmarks, most systems suffer noticeable performance drop in face of real-world speech mixtures due to severe domain mismatch. To address this, we introduce a novel context-aware two-stage training scheme with iterative updates for speech separation models. In this training scheme, the conventional end-to-end architecture is replaced with a framework that contains a context extractor and a segregator. The two modules are trained iteratively to emulate the speech-separation process of the human auditory system. We evaluate the proposed training scheme through cross-domain experiments on both synthetic and real-world speech mixtures, and demonstrate that the proposed scheme effectively boosts separation quality across various domains without adaptation, as measured by signal quality metrics and word error rate (WER). Additionally, our ablation study on the real test set highlights that the perceptual objects, including phoneme and word representations from pretrained self-supervised learning (SSL) models, serve as effective training targets for separation models.

title: Mitigating scalability challenges in LUT-based neural networks via pruning optimisations
authors: Xuqi Zhu, Huaizhi Zhang, JunKyu Lee, Jiacheng Zhu, Chandrajit Pal, Sangeet Saha, Klaus D. McDonald-Maier, Xiaojun Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computers
abstract: Modern deep neural networks heavily rely on a large number of multiply-accumulate operations, which constitute the predominant computational cost. To address this, Look-Up Table (LUT)-based matrix multiplications have emerged as a promising alternative for reducing the computational cost and time of the multiply-accumulate operations in a neural network. However, the LUT-based neural network still faces the scalability challenge due to the inherent limitations of LUT-based matrix multiplication. To mitigate these scalability limitations, this paper proposes a scalable and energy-efficient LUT-based approximate matrix multiplication unit (LUT-MU) constituting the basic component of the neural networks by integrating a pruning strategy on the MADDNESS algorithm, a LUT-based matrix multiplication methodology. With increasing problem size and precision demands in matrix multiplication, our proposed LUT-MU architecture effectively constrains resource expansion. The case study shows that deploying our LUT-MU in neural network architectures, including fully connected layers (MNIST) and ResNets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet)—on XCZU7EV and XCZU19EG FPGAs, produces up to 1.6× throughput improvement and 4.2× energy efficiency gains over mainstream CUDA-based network implementations, and 1.8× energy efficiency compared to leading quantised neural network implementations, with moderate impact on accuracy. Compared to original MADDNESS-based neural networks, our LUT-MU shows 1.3 to 2.6× resource savings based on various resolution configuration settings of MADDNESS.

title: Digital Twin Integrated Diffusion Graph Neural Network for Smart Respiratory Signal Analysis and Prediction
authors: A. Nivetha, Manikandan A, Divyadharshini A, Mithun Bahavath S K
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Continuous monitoring of the health of the respiratory system is critical in the early diagnosis and treatment of diseases related to the respiratory system. However, most of the respiratory health monitoring systems lack the features for providing flexibility and customizability in the process of analysis. This research work proposes a novel framework for the intelligent monitoring and classification of signals associated with the respiratory system using a digital twin and spatio-temporal graph neural networks to design an intelligent system. Audio cues collected from the respiratory system are segmented using a sliding window approach and advanced feature extraction methods such as Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients and their derivatives. A diffusion-based graph learning model is used as an effective way of modeling the complicated spatial and temporal connectivity between respiratory characteristics, which polishes signal representations by an iterative denoising procedure. Hypothesis Digital twin technology is designed to be integrated with advanced graphic diffusion learning to provide continuous monitoring, early diagnosis, and personalized healthcare services. Altogether, the suggested solution offers a viable and scalable one in the field of intelligent respiratory health monitoring in real-time medical applications.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Analysis of Asteroid Flexible Brush Sampling
authors: Taotao Hu, Fan Guo, He Zhang
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Sampling techniques for asteroid regolith represent a critical challenge in deep-space exploration missions. Flexible brush sampling technology has garnered significant attention due to its high efficiency and low disturbance characteristics. This paper systematically investigates the dynamic response and sampling performance of flexible sampling mechanisms in microgravity environments using a coupled simulation method integrating particle dynamics and flexible multibody dynamics. The reliability of the coupled model is validated through multisource verification using drop tower experimental data and literature results. Based on the validated model, the study delves into the impact of key parameters-including brush elastic modulus, damping ratio, mechanism feed rate, and rotational speed-on sampling efficiency. Results indicate that increasing the brush’s elastic modulus and rotational speed, while reducing the feed rate, effectively enhances sampling performance. Furthermore, multidimensional comparisons elucidate the differing sampling mechanisms between flexible and rigid brush mechanisms: the flexible brush periodically stores and releases strain energy, converting collision-dissipated energy from the rigid mechanism into kinetic energy for particle transport.

title: Automated Animal Intrusion Detection and Repellent Activation in Crop Fields
authors: Krithika S, Keerthi Sheevani G, Lekhavarshini V, Balaji M
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: This paper proposes an artificial intelligence– based system to monitor animal intrusion in agricultural fields. The proposed system uses a computer vision model implemented on a Raspberry Pi–based edge device to detect animals in real time. Once the animal is detected, the repellent activation is initiated to protect the fields. The animals considered in this work include monkeys, elephants, and wild boars. An IR motion sensor is placed all around the field and upon detection of movement, a camera module connected to the Raspberry Pi is activated. It captures images from the field and processes them using the trained detection model for animal classification. The performance of the classification algorithm has been analyzed using metrics that include precision, recall, and F1-score. The results indicate that the proposed system is capable of providing an effective and reliable solution for protecting agricultural fields.

title: Wide-Aperture Sensor for Atmospheric and Space Optical Quantum Communication Systems
authors: D. D. Chizhin, V. V. Toporovsky, A. Yu. Epifanov, I. V. Galaktionov, N. V. Pchelkina
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This paper presents a theoretical investigation and experimental analysis of wavefront sensors based on the ShackHartmann configuration and the Talbot effect. A comparative analysis of their characteristics indicates that the Talbot effectbased wavefront sensor is comparable to the Shack-Hartmann scheme in terms of input resolution, measurement frequency, and accuracy. The experiment demonstrates the feasibility of employing the Talbot effect-based wavefront sensor in adaptive optics systems for atmospheric and space optical communication channels, including those utilizing quantum key distribution technology.

title: Beyond Autotuning: A Gamified Learning Path for Deep Understanding of Control Systems
authors: Sergey Mezin, Anatoly Kosoy, Vladislav Belyaev
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: A significant gap exists between the theoretical study of automatic control and the practical skills required to tune regulators in real-world industrial settings, such as power plants. For safety reasons, students are prohibited from conducting experiments on live systems, even during internships. While mathematical software packages are used for training, they often provide an incomplete experience, typically involving ready-made transfer functions and idealized simulation environments. This paper presents a novel gamified training simulator designed to bridge this gap. The system is built around a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) that realistically simulates a technological process, coupled with an operator panel that allows connection of a student's laptop. The simulator imposes real-world constraints: experiments must stay within safe process limits, measurement data contains various noises (additive, multiplicative, outliers, drift), and the data acquisition rate emulates different controller grades (from 2 seconds to 0.1 seconds). Students must design experiments, properly filter and process data to identify a plant model, and then tune a regulator of arbitrary structure (P, PI, PID, positional, custom). The final evaluation is conducted not on the student's identified model, but on the "real" model inside the PLC. A scoring system based on setup time, number of alarms triggered, and key control quality indices fosters competition among students and against machine optimization algorithms. This approach, moving "beyond autotuning," creates a deep, practical, and engaging learning path for mastering control systems.

title: YOLOv11-Based Real-Time License Plate Detection and Recognition for Smart Transportation Systems
authors: Nitya J, Dharshini V, Fathima Jemima, Pradheesh P G, Tharun P M
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The smart transportation system includes intelligent license plate detection and recognition as one of the vital aspects since it allows real-time traffic monitoring, vehicle recognition, and urban mobility management based on the data. The YOLOv11-based models are fast and accurate enough to be applied to the traffic conditions of cities. Algorithms that are up to date tend to be cloud-reliant, slow at latency, do not generalize well to a variety of plate formats, and are computationally expensive, which limits their applicability in real-time traffic predictions and edge-based implementations. The suggested model uses a self-trained YOLOv11 architecture based on a custom dataset with a range of plate formats, light scenes, and traffic scenes. It is integrated with offline inference, optimization of feature extraction, and local SQLite-based storage with smart duplicate filtering to overcome the latency and scalability problems. This system aids in traffic monitoring, congestion, parking, and toll monitoring at the infrastructure. The experimental outcomes lead to better detection accuracy, a higher response time, and strong performance on the mid-range hardware, which allows effective traffic analytics. The results demonstrate that YOLOv-11 consistently outperforms Intuitionistic Fuzzy Programming (IFP), End-to-End Deep Learning (E2E-DL), and Deep Learning Framework (DLF) by achieving higher detection and recognition accuracy, lower inference latency, and superior traffic data reliability for real-time smart transportation systems.

title: A Hybrid VGG19–ResNet50 Feature Fusion Framework with Imbalance-Aware Learning for Robust Brain Tumor Classification
authors: SivaKumar Depuru, Mokkala Kiran Moni, Suvarna Yashaswini, Dudekula Pedda Madarsa, R Neeradh Kumar Reddy, P C Jaswanth
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: MRI scans continue to be a significant challenge in the accurate classification of brain tumors as high inter-class similarity, heterogeneity of tumors and grossly unbalanced classes of the complete set of classes make such classification difficult resulting in biased predictions in the majority classes. In order to overcome these shortcomings, this research proposes a hybrid deep learning architecture that combines VGG19 and ResNet50 by implementing feature-level fusion and learning on imbalanced data. The architecture integrates hierarchical spatial feature extraction of VGG19 with residual semantic learning of ResNet50 to get a more detailed representation of tumors. During training, focal loss and synthetic augmentation methods are used to reduce the imbalance in data. This model was tested on the BraTS2020 dataset with 3, 000 MRI slices of four tumor types. The experimental results show an overall accuracy of 88, macro F1-score of 85.5 and average AUC of 0.92 is more than standalone VGG19 (83.4% accuracy) and ResNet50 (84.7% accuracy). The results suggest that feature fusion in the forms of a hybrid is much more effective in enhancing the discriminative performance and reliability in the multi-class brain tumor classification.

title: Representation Geometry Preservation for Robust Neural Network Pruning
authors: Anastasia S. Raskopina, Tatiana M. Tatarnikova, Ren Jianan
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: Structural pruning is widely used to reduce the computational complexity of deep neural networks. However, most existing approaches rely on local importance criteria and do not explicitly analyze the internal geometry of learned representations. In this work, structured pruning is investigated from the perspective of representation geometry preservation. The study focuses on spectral characteristics of class similarity matrices and statistical properties of channel activations. An analysis of spectral entropy and condition number under different pruning levels demonstrates that structural simplification does not lead to spectral collapse or numerical instability of representations. The results show that pruning guided by activation variance preserves the global organization of class relationships while maintaining classification performance.

title: Simulation and Analysis of Starlight Transmission Through Martian Dust Aerosols
authors: Hu Kai, Zheng Xunjiang, Sun Shuodong, Zhang Xuwei, Jiang Lihui, Dai Feng
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Although the Martian atmosphere is thin, starlight is attenuated by periodic dust storms, which makes astronomical navigation techniques that depend on stellar measurements difficult to employ. This study used the optical properties of Martian dust to build a dust microparticle model and a spatiotemporal evolution model for regional dust storms in order to evaluate the effect of dust storms on starlight transmittance through the Martian atmosphere. The model simulated the distribution of dust particles, computed optical thickness over the visible to near-infrared spectrum, and used the Beer-Lambert law to examine changes in starlight transmittance under various dust concentrations, observation wavelengths, and zenith angles. Dust storms dramatically increase optical thickness, resulting in decreased transmission, according to numerical simulations. The attenuation shows a positive correlation with wavelength and a negative correlation with zenith angle and dust content. Transmittance at 400 nm and 1000 nm dropped from $87.4 \%$ and $89.8 \%$ during calm periods to $62.7 \%$ and $68.4 \%$, respectively, during peak dust storm intensity at an observation zenith angle of 0. Transmittance further dropped to $36.3 \%$ and $52.5 \%$ at a zenith angle of 60°. Additionally, transmission decreased more at short wavelengths than at long wavelengths. These findings suggest that during dust storms, attenuation is more noticeable for lowelevation stars and short-wavelength photons. For upcoming Mars exploration missions, the results offer theoretical guidance for developing observation plans, choosing the best wavelength bands, and building astronomical navigation systems.

title: Trustworthy Autonomous RAN Orchestration: An Architectural Framework for Zero-Trust Intent-Based Control with Explainable AI
authors: Mukesh Dua, Tarun Kundu, Ankush Gupta
publish: 2026 35th Wireless and Optical Communications Conference (WOCC)
abstract: As sixth-generation (6G) networks move toward more autonomous orchestration, operators face a key challenge where they need to use AI-driven, intent-based systems across different vendors, but they do not have clear ways to verify how policies are created, review decision logic, or build trust without sharing sensitive network data. We introduce a framework combining zero-trust principles with intent-based RAN orchestration, explainable AI (XAI), and federated threat detection. The framework is built on four layers: (1) Intent Translation, where LLM agents decompose high-level intents into domain rules with full reasoning provenance; (2) Zero-Trust Validation, which checks policies against 3GPP standards [1], [2], [3], history, and network state before deployment; (3) Federated Trust Coordination, which enables edge-trained threat detection while keeping raw data private; and (4) Auditable Orchestration, that links every decision to its source, model version, and fallback options. We also lay out architectural patterns that help multi-vendor RICs work together, explain how to detect model drift before it becomes a problem, and offer safe ways for autonomous networks to explore and learn on their own. Instead of relying on opaque, black-box automation, this framework builds AI-driven RAN control on clear verification, transparent reasoning, and distributed trust, giving operators the confidence they need to deploy next-generation networks.

title: Designing of Mobile-Assisted Foreign Language Learning System
authors: Natalia Alexandrovna Kopylova, Shamil Alievich Otsokov, Marina Anatolievna Slepneva
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: The integration of rapidly evolving digital technologies is transforming our life, including education, thus necessitating new pedagogical approaches and reviewing the methodology of teaching disciplines. The aim of this study was to design an innovative approach – a mobile-assisted foreign language learning system partly built on artificial intelligence and deployed via a popular messenger platform. The authors present the architecture of one of the options for teaching grammar in the language messenger channel using the example of the English tenses system in the form of a goal tree, the idea of which is used to represent knowledge as a hierarchy of tasks or concepts necessary for mastering. The application developed to improve E-learning in a foreign language will be realized in the work of a technical university, which will improve the quality of education and increase the students’ interest in learning a foreign language for professional purposes. A preliminary survey was conducted among the teaching staff of the Foreign Languages Department on the effectiveness and relevance of applying such technologies in the educational process. According to the survey results, the majority of respondents supported the digital technologies’ usage and saw no obstacles to their implementation.

title: Software Systems Design-Stage Sustainability Evaluation using Flow-Diagram-based Metrics
authors: Nivya Shree P, Gokila S
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Software systems are becoming an increasingly larger part of the world due to their energy consumption, and studies show that sustainability is an afterthought when implementing software, making it difficult to redesign. Efforts at the design level such as complicated control flows, repeated validation logic, deep nesting and multiple checkpoints can cause various problems for the sustainability of software. The goal of this research work is to propose a model for analyzing the sustainability of software before it is coded using conventional software engineering processes by applying metrics to flow diagrams to create a Design Sustainability Score (DSS) at the design level. The DSS was determined using three separate CRUD-based applications: Note Management (Note), Student Management (Student) and Address Book Management (Address). The results indicated that the proposed GreenStream Design had a higher DSS than the original design.

title: Aerial Pursuit: Multi-Agent Swarm for Predator–Prey Games
authors: Wu Zining, Chen Haoran, Zhao Jinqiu, Peng Yifeng, Sutthiphong Srigrarom, Boo Cheong Khoo
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This paper addresses the cold-start and convergence challenges of multi-agent adversarial reinforcement learning in physics-rich environments. The UAVs are modeled as predator-prey agents inspired by eagle-hen interactions. We propose a curriculum-based two-stage training scheme: Stage 1 trains the hens against a heuristic eagle, and Stage 2 freezes the learned hen policy while training the eagle. Using Box2D physics and Gymnasium interfaces, we build a reproducible engineering pipeline based on Stable-Baselines3 PPO, parallel sampling, and TensorBoard logging. Our main contributions include environment modeling (world construction, chain joints, boundary handling, and bounce dynamics), observation and reward design, parallel training with evaluation callbacks, and behavior visualization with metric analysis. The results show improved defensive behavior by the hens in Stage 1, reflected in increasing mean returns, explained variance approaching 1, and decreasing entropy loss. In Stage 2, the eagle learns effective approach and capture behaviors, producing positive potential-based rewards and sparse positive returns upon capture while maintaining smooth overall training curves. Remaining limitations include the empirical tuning of reward weights and physical parameters. Future work will explore joint fine-tuning, self-play, and more complex scenarios.

title: Polyamide based Flexible Logo Antenna with Excellent Impedance match for Wearable Applications
authors: Manikandan Manoharan, Aravinthkumar Veeramanikandan, Gopinath Senthilkumar, Jegatheesh Karuppusamy
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: A printed logo slot antenna operating in the ISM band for wearable applications is proposed in this article. The proposed antenna is bears a resemblance to the logo of SSM Institute of Engineering and Technology, Dindigul. This approach demonstrates the feasibility of incorporating institutional identity into highly functional RF components. Printing an institutional logo using copper material on a thin, flexible polyamide substrate having thickness of 1 mm is the major novelty of the proposed design. This novelty makes the proposed design ideal for conformal applications such as wearable electronics and flexible communication devices. The dimension of the proposed antenna is 46.82 x 42.49 x 1.07 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> which is engineered to operate at a resonant frequency of 2.448 GHz that falls in ISM band. Simulation results validate the electrical performance of proposed antenna, highlighted by an outstanding return loss (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf>) of -42.25 dB, signifying near perfect impedance matching. Consequently, the antenna achieves an ultra-low VSWR of 1.01 and delivers a peak gain of 4.65 dBi. The combination of mechanical stability, compact size, high gain and superior impedance confirms that the logo-based geometry on the polyamide substrate is a robust and efficient solution for next-generation flexible wireless communication systems.

title: QLoRA Fine-Tuning for Next User Turn Prediction and Multi-Step Dialogue Rollouts
authors: Sebastian Boehler, Tianxiang Lu
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Most dialogue research optimizes assistant responses, leaving user-turn prediction underexplored. We investigate quantized low-rank adaptation fine-tuning of four open-source instruction-tuned large language models spanning 1.2B to 7B parameters for generating the next user utterance from multi-turn dialogue context. Models are trained on 6,400 samples from two complementary corpora, one open-domain and one task-oriented, and evaluated against pretrained models, GPT-4o-mini prompt baselines; all models are assessed using automated metrics, short multi-step rollouts, and a three-rater blinded human study of 369 samples. Fine-tuning consistently improves fluency and semantic quality across three of four model families, with Llama-3B achieving the highest single-turn performance. Reference-assisted rollouts show stable three-step behavior for the strongest fine-tuned models. Human evaluation confirms a 0.37 average improvement on a 5 point scale, with task-oriented dialogue benefiting more (0.46) than open-domain conversation (0.26). A systematic ablation identifies a strong LoRA configuration on the 1.2B model, while OLMo-7B follow-up sweep does not recover base-model semantic quality. Our results show that fine-tuning enables practical and reproducible user-turn prediction, and that short rollout evaluation is necessary to separate single-turn gains from longerhorizon stability.

title: Applying DevOps and DaaS in the Smart Laboratory of Internet of Things
authors: Ilya Izotov, Natalya Papulovskaya
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: This paper presents the results of developing a Smart Laboratory. The Smart Laboratory is a platform for remotely developing embedded system software. It enables remote access to physical hardware for software development, testing, and deployment on microcontroller-based devices. The creation of the online laboratory involved several key tasks: designing a network infrastructure to integrate laboratory equipment into a unified system, developing a web application for remote interaction with microcontrollers, implementing a digital twin of the test module for flexible configuration and management, and conducting testing and validation. During development, a hypothesis emerged that the combination of DevOps and Device as a Service (DaaS) opens new possibilities for automated IoT device management. The DaaS concept enables on-demand remote access to physical devices, while the DevOps methodology facilitated the automation of user software deployment. A compilation and delivery pipeline for firmware updates was implemented to support this process. Ansible was utilized to automate software updates on Raspberry Pi testbed computers. Prometheus was employed for metric collection, and Grafana was used for data visualization, ensuring system performance monitoring. The platform was tested and validated at the Institute of Radio Electronics and Information Technologies - RTF (UrFU). The invention has been patented.

title: An Intelligent Smart Logistics and Cold-Chain Monitoring Framework Using the Coligo Platform
authors: Balaji Doraiswamy M, Navin Sathiabal, Keerthika Velusamy, Aishwarya Lakshmi M K, Dharshini Priya J
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: An IoT-based cold chain monitoring solution whose main aim is to guarantee the safety and quality of temperature-sensitive products. Cold chain supply chains need constant and stable temperature monitoring since any slight variations may damage the quality of the product and result in losses in finances. The paper meets this requirement to create a Coligo Cold Chain Monitoring System based on IoT sensors attached to an ESP32 microcontroller installed in an ice cream truck. The system will also periodically take temperature readings in the refrigerated storage cabinet and send the information on the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport communication protocol in real time. MQTT is highly appropriate in these types of applications since it is light, fast, and reliable even in an environment where the connectivity is unstable. The data sent is then transmitted to Coligo Lite a monitoring platform which displays the temperature readings and raises an alarm in case of values beyond predetermined levels. This enables operators to easily identify abnormal conditions and take corrective measures before damage is caused to products. The outcomes of the experiment indicate that the system can provide a stable transmission of data and correct real-time monitoring alongside dependable alarm generation. Also, the system architecture is scalable and flexible. It may be improved with the addition of more sensors, a mobile app notification, and an automated temperature control system. In sum, the proposed system is a viable and cost-effective, to enhance the transparency, reliability, and quality assurance of the contemporary cold supply chains.

title: The Impact of Satisfaction, Perceived Interactivity, and Learning Value on ChatGPT Use Intention and Behavior Among Indonesian Zoomers Students
authors: Lianna Wijaya, Sudip Wagle, Hazlina Abd Kadir, Anurika Vaish, Rakhdiny Sustaningrum, Reinandus Aditya Gunawan, The Elisabeth Cintya Santosa, Rininta Ayu Pradhani
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The growing use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools like ChatGPT is increasingly disrupting higher education, notably for Generation Z students in Indonesia. This research investigates the effect of Perceived Interactivity, Perceived Satisfaction, Perceived Innovativeness, and Perceived Learning Value on ChatGPT Use Intention (UIT) and Use Behavior (UBV) in an educational context. A quantitative method was applied whereby data was obtained by distributing a survey to 307 Gen Z students between March and November 2025. Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) was conducted through SmartPLS 4.0. The results reveal that Perceived Satisfaction and Perceived Learning Value significantly impact students' Use Intention, confirming their critical role in technology adoption. Perceived Innovativeness and Perceived Interactivity showed insignificant effects, suggesting that innovation and interactivity alone may not drive adoption in this context. Furthermore, Use Intention was found to have a dominant influence on actual Use Behavior, highlighting its importance in bridging perceptions with actual usage. This study contributes to extending the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) by incorporating experiential factors such as interactivity and learning value. The findings offer practical implications for developers and educators, emphasizing the need to enhance user satisfaction and learning outcomes to foster greater ChatGPT adoption. In future studies, researchers could explore these elements in diverse cultural and educational settings.

title: Confidence-Aware Dynamic Modality Weighting for Robust and Explainable Multimodal Breast Cancer Risk Assessment
authors: Pearl Rubyth Thomas R, Ralitsa Narayan, Tejasri S, T. Grace Shalini
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Standard medical multimodal AI systems apply static fusion techniques with fixed weights that are unable to adjust whenever data quality changes or if certain modalities are missing. This limitation results in the complete breakdown of trustworthiness for the decision support system. The proposed Confidence-Aware Dynamic Fusion (CADF) framework dynamically modifies modality weights during inference based on calculated confidence levels. CADF establishes trust in modality weights based on their predictive accuracy throughout the testing procedure. When evaluating the imaging modalities, CADF incorporates entropy-based confidence metrics and SHAP stability metrics to evaluate trustworthiness of genomic data used to estimate modality weights. The developed method also allows the system to make weight adjustment without retraining and provide stable numerical output during events in which modalities fail. Applications of CADF to a multimodal breast cancer dataset demonstrated enhanced calibration and robustness of CADF compared to conventional approaches, suggesting that CADF has the capability to provide an interpretable and effective streamlined clinical decision support tool for physicians. Based on testing performed during this research, CADF produced a mean AUC-ROC of 0.91 ±0.01 and a mean ECE of 0.034 ±0.005.

title: IEEE Recommended Practice for Ethical Considerations of Emulated Empathy in Partner-Based General-Purpose Artificial Intelligence Systems
authors: N/A
publish: N/A
abstract: Recommended practices for ethical usage of emulated empathy in general-purpose artificial intelligence (GPAI) systems for human-artificial intelligence (AI) partnerships are provided in this standard. The use of emulated empathy in GPAI systems for human-AI partnerships refers to general-purpose AI products marketed as empathic partners, personal AI, companions, co-pilots, agents, assistants, and related phrasing for human-AI partnering. IEEE Std 7014.1™ is a domainspecific extension of IEEE Std 7014™, IEEE Standard for Ethical Considerations in Emulated Empathy in Autonomous and Intelligent Systems. Whereas IEEE Std 7014 provides overarching principles for ethical evaluation of emulated empathy across AI, IEEE Std 7014.1 focuses on the overlap of GPAI, emulated empathy and human-AI partnering.

title: Governing Ai Without Adequate Institutions: Markets, Tort, and Ethics Under Deep Uncertainty
authors: Alex Atanasov
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Artificial intelligence governance is commonly framed as a problem of ethics, regulation, or liability design. This paper argues that the difficulty runs deeper: contemporary institutions fail because AI operates under conditions that disrupt the informational foundations on which governance depends. The article reconceptualizes tort law as an informational learning system whose effectiveness relies on epistemic stability-repeatable harms, attributable responsibility, and the accumulation of precedent over time. Advanced AI undermines these conditions through opacity, non-stationarity, and distributed agency, producing disputes that are difficult to interpret and resistant to doctrinal learning. Markets likewise struggle to internalize systemic risks under deep uncertainty and information asymmetry, while ethical frameworks are human-centred and lack enforcement, institutional memory, and coordination capacity. These failures are not independent but mutually reinforcing, generating governance that produces activity without durable constraint. The paper advances a broader claim: current AI governance operates in a pre-constitutional condition, in which no institution can reliably generate or stabilize authoritative interpretations of responsibility. Effective AI governance therefore requires institutional design capable of validating safety claims, allocating responsibility across complex systems, and sustaining interpretation under uncertainty, rather than further reliance on ethics alone.

title: The Impact of Green HRM Practices on Environmental Organizational Citizenship Behaviors: The Mediating Role of Organizational Culture
authors: Marwan Thakir Abed Aldosiry, Zainab Tawfeq. A, Basim Faisal Abdul Nayef, Sakher. Alnajdawi
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: This study objectives of the study is to identify the extent to which green HRM practices are adopted in the Ministry of Health, Jordan, to understand the current state of environmental organizational citizenship behaviors, to identify the presence of organizational culture, to test the impact of green HRM practices on strengthening organizational citizenship behaviors, to demonstrate the impact of green HRM practices on organizational culture, to find out the impact of environmental organizational citizenship behaviors on organizational culture, to find the impact of green HRM on strengthening environmental organizational citizenship behaviors through organizational culture as a mediating variable and to present a set of recommendations and proposals that would contribute to strengthening environmental organizational citizenship behaviors through the application of green HRM practices. The methodology used is the descriptive-analytical method was employed. This method relies on studying the phenomenon as it exists in reality, describing it accurately and expressing it both qualitatively and quantitatively. The study population refers to all individuals representing the phenomenon under study, sharing one or more specific characteristics. Data collection is required for this population. Accordingly, the study population was defined as the employees of the ministry of health. The study reveals several recommendation to practical entities.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence Framework for Real-Time Threat Detection in Smart Cybersecurity Systems
authors: Jayalakshmi R, Reeja Jose Pavanathil, Swetha C V, Jitha Janardhanan, Priya Hari, Rajalekshmi Srinivasan
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The integration of Explainable Artificial Intelligence frameworks into real-time cyber threat detection systems to strengthen transparency, trust, and operational effectiveness. The modern deep learning systems show high skills in detecting and predicting cyber attacks in sophisticated online systems. Such systems are still opaque in their practice and restrict the confidence of practitioners and the capability of these systems to be used effectively in Security Operation Centres. Low interpretability undermines situational awareness, lowers the level of confidence in decision making as well as collaboration between analysts and intelligent detection platforms. In this paper, explanatory mechanisms of system-on-a-chip interaction have been assessed by using Shapley Additive Explanations, Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations, and attention-based SHAP visualizations. The research focuses on the impact of explanatory output on the IT event interpretation and the process of decision-making in IT by using studies of security analysts and simulation-based evaluations. Results have shown that structured explanations increase the level of understanding among analysts of threats predictions, quality of decisions made and confidence of the automated results when time is of essence in incidents. Explanatory interfaces increase awareness of operations and assist in making informed human oversight in extreme response situations. The analysis also examines the autonomy paradox of cybersecurity automation, which is concerned with the connection between rationale presentation and responsibility assignment. The findings build upon current models of trust and trust by establishing XAI as a building block of practitioner cybersecurity systems. This framework helps to develop collaborative, adaptive, and human compatible threat detection designs to changing digital threat environments.

title: A Decoupled GRU-ELM Framework For Edge-Based Panel-Level Fault Detection in Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Mohanraj S, Balaguru Madhavan G, Chiradeep P, Magudeeshwari V, Shifana S
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Most traditional PV monitoring systems use the string level or inverter based measurements. This leads to the local performance loss at specific modules being frequently obscured and thus causing a challenge for fault detection. To overcome this shortcoming, in this paper we propose an edgebased intelligent panel-level fault detection framework that fuses multi-sensor measurements using a decoupled deep learning–machine learning architecture. Here the system measures the solar irradiance, panel temperature, output voltage and output current at panel level and executes localized preprocessing on edge node. The preprocessed multi sensor time-series data is used as input to a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) network dedicated entirely for temporal feature extraction, which allows for the effective modeling of degradation profiles and dynamic operating conditions. The obtained temporal features are then fed into an ELM, which is beneficial to computation efficiency and maintains fault discrimination ability as compared to traditional end-to-end deep classifiers. Several panel operating points are addressed by the framework, among others normal operation, dust induced efficiency decrease and temperature related loss of performance as well as electric fault cases. Through disentangling temporal representation learning and classification, this hybrid GRU–ELM architecture allows low-latency, resource-effective fault detection in real-time edge with minimal computational support from the cloud. The approach allows for low cost scaling and utilization on rooftop as well as utility-scale PV installations. These traits make it highly compatible with new generation panel-level solar monitoring systems.

title: Urllc Scheduling Under Latency Constraints Using Stochastic Network Calculus: Improved Analysis and Simulation
authors: T Haritha, K.K. Manivannan, Mahip Aggarwal, Jayapal Lande
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: 5G/6G networks with ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) require packet delays of less than a millisecond with failure probabilities on the order of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10{-5}$</tex>. Achieving this level of quality of service (QoS) requires admission and scheduling strategies that account for the probability of service and the scarcity of radio resources. Stochastic network calculus (SNC) can be used to compute bounds on the probability that packets violate delay constraints in queueing systems, while recent data-driven and learning-based scheduling techniques can be used to adapt to changing scenarios. This paper revisits constrained latency scheduling for URLLC using SNC and simulation. This document provides an overview of the state-of-the-art on SNC-based models, scheduling for mixed traffic, NOMA-assisted URLLC, URLLC scheduling in industrial networks formulated as a Markov decision process, energy-efficient and data-driven scheduling, link adaptation based on reinforcement learning, and emerging methods such as local-deadline partitioning. Then, the SNC delay bound (based on the moment-generating function, MGF) for a priority queue is derived and applied to calculate an upper bound on the delay violation probability of a URLLC packet subject to a 1 ms deadline. A synthetic non-preemptive priority queue simulator is developed to obtain statistical delay and reliability performance at different arrival rates. Simulations demonstrate that the SNC bound provides a conservative but useful bound and that the probability of exceeding 1 ms delay rises sharply as URLLC arrival rate approaches service rate. The results from the simulation reveal the trade-off between efficiency and reliability, which can be used to schedule flows of URLLC traffic in future networks.

title: Robust Fault-Tolerant Control for Dual-Drone Slung Load Systems Under Rotor Damage
authors: Xiaoyu Yi, Ziya Jiang, Yu Tian, Xiaolong Zhu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This paper proposes an integrated hierarchical fault-tolerant control framework to address the control challenges of a dual-drone cooperative slung load system under partial rotor failure. The primary contributions of this work are twofold: 1) A hierarchical robust controller with a combined inner-outer loop structure is constructed to achieve precise position tracking and load swing suppression; 2) A finite-time disturbance observer is designed for each drone to actively compensate for the lumped disturbances caused by the fault. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively estimates and compensates for fault-induced disturbances, ensuring system stability and minimizing payload oscillation even under severe rotor damage conditions, thereby verifying the outstanding contribution of the proposed framework in improving system robustness.

title: An Accuracy and Computational Performance Study of Basis, Operator, and Curvilinear Implementations of DGTD for Maxwell’s equations
authors: Alejandro Muñoz Manterola, Luis Manuel Díaz Angulo, Alberto Gascón Bravo, Carlos Julio Ramos Salas, Farah Amador, Salvador González García
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: We present an accuracy and computational-performance study of Discontinuous Galerkin Time-Domain (DGTD) variants for Maxwell’s equations. The comparison covers: (i) open Legendre–Gauss (LG) versus closed Legendre–Gauss–Lobatto (LGL) nodal bases, (ii) a fully assembled global sparse evolution operator versus a matrix-light connectivity-based (Hesthaven-inspired) operator, and (iii) linear versus second-order curvilinear elements. Validation is performed on three representative benchmarks: (A) a 2D PEC cavity excited with mode TM55, (B) a 2D circular cavity initialized with a Bessel J6 mode, and (C) the 3D monostatic Radar Cross-Section (RCS) of a PEC sphere to evaluate curved-element benefits against an analytical Mie-series reference. Beyond error convergence, the benchmark sections report representative sparsity through the number of non-zero entries (NNZ), together with the runtime and memory indicators relevant to each comparison. The results show that open and closed bases deliver comparable field accuracy where they are directly compared, while LGL yields systematically sparser operators and, in the reported TM55 and 3D RCS runs, lower memory/runtime cost in the studied high-order regimes. For TM55, open-versus-closed temporal-evolution memory differences reach about 64% at the highest spatially refined case (H3) with a basis order of p = 10, and global-versus-local memory differences reach about 88% in favor of the local operator at the same configuration. Curvilinear elements are essential to remove geometry-induced error floors in curved-domain benchmarks; their computational overhead is benchmark- and configuration-dependent rather than universal. These results provide practical guidance for selecting bases, operators, and geometric representations in large-scale, highfidelity time-domain electromagnetics.

title: Attention-Driven Alaryngeal Speech Enhancement via Discrete Representation Learning and Timbre-Preserving Augmentation
authors: Hnin Yadana Lwin, Wuttipong Kumwilaisak, Chatchawarn Hansakunbuntheung, Nattanun Thatphithakkul
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Alaryngeal speech enhancement seeks to restore intelligibility and naturalness for individuals who have undergone laryngectomy. Existing methods based on direct spectral mapping or frame-level voice conversion fail to exploit speech's linguistic structure and are constrained by scarce pathological data. We propose a multi-stage framework integrating discrete representation learning and attention-driven feature alignment. WavLM embeddings are quantized via <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-means and refined by a Speech Language Model that maps alaryngeal tokens toward normal-speech tokens, followed by Conformer-based reconstruction. An Attention-Driven Feature Matcher (ADFM) then refines features through three coupled modules: a residual Feature Enhancer with learnable scaling, a Multi-Head Attention Matcher performing soft alignment over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math></inline-formula>-nearest normal-speech neighbors, and a Quality Predictor that estimates per-frame confidence as an inference-time reliability indicator. A HiFi-GAN vocoder synthesizes the waveform. To overcome data scarcity, we introduce a Multi-Mask GAN augmentation strategy extending MaskCycleGAN-VC with a timbre similarity loss for speaker-identity preservation. Evaluations on Thai and English corpora show best speaker similarity in both languages (0.858 Thai, 0.850 English) with competitive or state-of-the-art FAD (5.252 Thai, 5.385 English) and MCD (5.644 Thai, 5.322 English), yielding up to 27.3% FAD reduction over kNN-VC. Subjective tests confirm significant listener preference (77% English, 67% Thai).

title: Safety-Certified Cooperative Path Following of Networked Autonomous Surface Vehicles Based on Nash Equilibrium Seeking With Experiments
authors: Yanping Xu, Lu Liu, Bing Han, Anqing Wang, Zhouhua Peng
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: This article addresses the safety-certified cooperative path following of networked underactuated autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) with individual interests navigating complex marine environments. Each ASV operates under static/dynamic obstacle constraints, neighboring ASV constraints, and input constraints. A three-layer safety-certified path-following architecture is proposed based on control barrier functions and Nash equilibrium seeking. At the communication layer, a path variable update law based on distributed Nash equilibrium seeking is designed to achieve path variable cooperation. At the nominal layer, a line-of-sight-based principle is employed to design guidance laws for each ASV to achieve path following. At the optimization layer, three heading-constrained control barrier functions are developed for mapping safety constraints to constraints on yaw rates of ASVs, and a quadratic program is constructed to optimize the guidance yaw rate signal. The distinct features of the proposed method are two aspects: each ASV is allowed to consider its individual interest when performing a group task; static/dynamic obstacles and neighboring ASVs can be avoided by changing the yaw rate only. Stability analysis demonstrates that the feedback control system exhibits input-to-state stability. The efficacy of the novel method is validated through both hardware-in-the-loop simulation results and field experiment results.

title: Open-Circuit Fault Diagnosis of Voltage Source Inverters Based on Microfeature Extraction and Third-Order Tracking Differentiator
authors: Hongyi Guo, Chonghui Song, Zhengkuo Jiao, Zhenjie Xu, Qiuyang Xu, Yucong Zhang, Shuo Li, Naizhe Diao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes an open-circuit (OC) fault diagnosis method for voltage source inverters (VSIs) based on microfeature extraction and a third-order tracking differentiator. The proposed method is designed for complex operating conditions such as variable frequency and variable load. It enables effective diagnosis of both types I and II faults. First, a PWM-aligned masked averaging filter (PMAF) is developed. By constructing two averaging windows within each carrier period and masking the intermediate interval, the proposed filter effectively suppresses noise while preserving fault features. Second, a third-order linear tracking differentiator (LTD3) is employed to extract the second-order derivative of currents. The fault features are mapped into a unified second-order derivative domain. As a result, a unified fault criterion is established, eliminating the need for type-specific detection logic. On this basis, a unified fault diagnosis function is constructed to achieve fault detection and fault localization. Compared with existing methods, the proposed method shows improved diagnostic response speed, and maintains robustness under complex operating conditions, with an algorithmic complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bm{O}\mathbf{(1)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. Both simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Temporal-State-Switching of Self-Sweeping Fiber Lasers
authors: Kaile Wang, Zifan Li, Ping Wang, Zengrun Wen, Tian Cao
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: The self-sweeping effect originates from dynamic gratings established when a doped fiber within a laser resonator is subjected to a standing-wave field. In the temporal domain, this effect can be classified into two distinct regimes: the microsecond-pulse (MP) state and the continuous-wave (CW) state, with the principal criterion of differentiation being whether the doped fiber responsible for dynamic grating formation is pump-excited. Elucidating the underlying generation mechanisms of these two self-sweeping laser regimes and realizing controllable switching between their temporal states constitute a critical research focus in this field. In this work, a CW self-sweeping output was achieved by inducing dynamic gratings in an unpumped doped fiber (UDF) incorporated within a unidirectional ring cavity, and controllable transition from the CW regime to the MP self-sweeping regime was subsequently realized by introducing pump excitation to the UDF. Systematic experiments were conducted under four conditions by independently regulating the pump powers of the gain-doped fiber (GDF) and UDF (pumping only GDF/only UDF, fixing GDF pump power to adjust UDF power, fixing UDF pump power to adjust GDF power). Furthermore, a theoretical model of the CW self-sweeping laser was formulated based on rate equations. By systematically varying the gain of the UDF, the transition mechanism from CW to MP self-sweeping operation was elucidated. Both theoretical and experimental results demonstrate that progressively increasing the UDF pump power induces a regime transition of the laser from the CW state to the MP state, accompanied by a pronounced enhancement of the pulse repetition rate throughout the transition. This investigation overcomes the inherent limitation of conventional lasers restricted to a single temporal operating mode, achieves controllable switching between CW and MP outputs, and thereby provides a novel paradigm for the development of high-performance self-sweeping laser sources.

title: Receiver-Tilt-Robust RSS-Based VLP System Using a Normal Orthogonal Three-PD (NO3PD) Positioning Scheme
authors: Zhengliang Wang, Hang Xu, Aiying Yang, Yaojun Qiao
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: In received signal strength (RSS)-based visible light positioning (VLP) systems, receiver tilt directly affects the measured RSS and introduces bias into positioning estimates. To mitigate this issue, a normal orthogonal three-PD (NO3PD) structure is proposed to receive the positioning signal, where three PDs are arranged with mutually perpendicular normals. Theoretically, using the root- mean-square (RMS) of the RSS values from the NO3PD receiver for positioning eliminates the influence of tilt. To further reduce the complexity of the RSS extraction method, a cross-correlation-computed RSS (cccRSS) extraction method is proposed. To validate the robustness of the proposed NO3PD-based scheme against receiver tilt, we compare it with a single-PD-based scheme through simulations and experiments. Simulation results show that in a 100 cm × 100 cm × 160 cm space with the receiver tilt angle ranging from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$15^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, the NO3PD-based scheme achieves a maximum average positioning error of 2.91 cm with a range of 0.39 cm, while the single-PD-based scheme achieves 4.17 cm and 1.78 cm, respectively. Experimental results in a similarly sized space, with the receiver tilt angle ranging from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$13.1^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, show that the NO3PD-based scheme attains a maximum average positioning error of 4.67 cm and a range of 0.98 cm, compared to 6.78 cm and 3.37 cm for the single-PD scheme. To assess the time performance of cccRSS extraction method, we implement the method on a microcontroller unit (MCU) and compare it with a fast Fourier transform (FFT)-based method, showing that it consumes only 6.4 % of the computation time of the latter. Furthermore, at a receiver speed of 35 cm/s without tilt, real-time positioning using the NO3PD-based scheme achieves an average positioning error of 3.93 cm, which is consistent with the previous error. Overall, the proposed NO3PD-based scheme exhibits smaller error ranges under tilt variation and achieves centimeter-level real-time positioning, demonstrating its superior tilt robustness and real-time capability.

title: Cross-Layer Throughput Analysis of Hybrid-Access Wi-Fi in Time-Varying Channels
authors: S. Arthi, Neelesh B. Mehta
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: Conventional Wi-Fi standards employ contention-based access, in which a user adapts the modulation and coding scheme (MCS) based on slow medium access control (MAC)-layer feedback. On the other hand, the current-generation IEEE 802.11ax standard employs hybrid access, in which the access point (AP) uses trigger frames to schedule users over orthogonal resource units (RUs) and controls their MCSs in a more agile manner to achieve a higher spectral efficiency. We develop a novel, cross-layer analysis that accurately characterizes the saturation throughput of hybrid access in time-varying channels. Unlike the literature, our approach accounts for channel state information (CSI) acquisition delays and their dependence on RU allocation and the number of scheduled users, which vary with time. Our results reveal the extent to which the CSI delays degrade the throughput due to the timescale of operation of the 802.11ax MAC. We also propose a throughput-optimal MCS selection rule that effectively mitigates the impact of CSI delays.

title: A Generalized Precise Modeling Framework for High-Step-Up Resonant Converters
authors: Qianxiang Shen, Enhui Chu, Pengcheng Wang, Chenghao Sun, Qiuye Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: To achieve miniaturization and high step-up in resonant converters, high resonant frequencies and high transformer turns ratios are required, resulting in small primary magnetizing inductance and large secondary winding capacitance. This leads to the transition from mainstream three-element resonant topologies (e.g., LLC, LCC) to more complex four element resonant modes, for which existing analytical methods become inadequate. This paper proposes a generalized precise modeling framework for such high-resonant-frequency high step-up (HRF-HSU) converters. The framework abstracts the resonant behavior of four elements into sequences of two fixed operational stages, standardizing the modeling process across the entire switching frequency range. Models derived from this framework accurately describe resonant dynamics in any frequency band and precisely plot full-range gain-frequency characteristics. The accuracy of the proposed modeling framework is assessed against experimental data and compared with that of mainstream modeling methods, demonstrating its advantage in reducing error. Finally, using the calculated gain frequency curves and resonant tank state trajectories, a case study on switching frequency range selection and resonant parameter optimization is presented.

title: A Compact High-Performance MR Safe Pneumatic Stepper Motor
authors: Ziting Liang, Ryman Hashem, Chuang Lu, Mohamed E.M.K. Abdelaziz, Steve Bandula, Vincent Groenhuis, Stefano Stramigioli, Danail Stoyanov, Agostino Stilli
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-guided interventions have advanced rapidly due to their superior soft-tissue contrast and real-time imaging capability. To enable actuation for robots and other medical devices used in tumour biopsy and ablation within magnetically sensitive environments, several MRI-compatible pneumatic motors have been developed using 3D printing. Although 3D printing enables the fabrication of pneumatic structures, maintaining airtightness and smooth piston motion within 3D-printed cylinders remains challenging due to tolerance variations and surface roughness. These issues lead to pressure losses, increased air consumption, and reduced torque output during motor operation. To address these challenges, this paper presents a compact, high-performance, easily manufacturable MR Safe pneumatic stepper motor that circumvents 3D-printing tolerance limitations by employing prefabricated cylinders. The motor combines a replaceable design with great airtightness and high torque performance. The proposed design achieves negligible air leakage of 0.047 mL/min measured via a designed water displacement method and a maximum output torque of 1.04 Nm at 3.7 revolutions per minute (rpm) under 6 bar, without an additional gearbox. The motor has a compact design, 48 × 48 × 36 mm, which enables the motor to be integrated into the medical devices within the narrow MRI scanning bore.

title: Deep Uncertainty-Aware Tracking for Maneuvering Targets
authors: Shuyang Zhang, Chang Gao, Bo Chen, Qingfu Zhang, Shefeng Yan, Hongwei Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Traditional target tracking approaches typically presuppose a target motion model. Once the target maneuvers, the model may get mismatched and is incapable of depicting the complex motion characteristics of the target. Most data-driven methods are designed based on a regression-only framework, which increases the difficulty of network training. Moreover, these methods are typically designed under the assumption of a fixed measurement noise distribution. However, the measurement noise distribution of actual radar is influenced by fluctuations in the target's radar cross section. Such fluctuations may lead to a degradation in tracking performance. In this paper, we propose a representation method for radar measurements, referred to as the measurement uncertainty projection operator (MUPO). It projects the measurement onto a target state space to capture the measurement noise distribution. Additionally, we propose a framework that reformulates maneuvering target tracking as a hybrid task of classification and regression. To realize the functionality of this framework, we establish a MUPO-based target tracking network. The rationality of the proposed network structure is supported by the validation experiment. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior estimation precision compared with existing data-driven methods during target maneuvers. Furthermore, the proposed algorithm retains its superior tracking performance when extended to 3D maneuvering target tracking and multi-target tracking scenarios.

title: Method of Autonomous Quality Evaluation for Space Remote Sensing Processing Data Based on Transfer Learning
authors: Zhigang Wu, Luosi Liu, Haochen He
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: Intelligent processing techniques for space remote sensing data are currently facing technical bottlenecks, leading to issues such as blurred and distorted images, low confidence levels in detection and recognition, and difficulties in direct use of the processed data. To address these challenges, incorporating quality evaluation information into space remote sensing processing data can enable users to refer to evaluation results and adopt flexible data usage strategies, thereby significantly improving the effectiveness and flexibility of utilizing such data. To enhance the accuracy and timeliness of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data, there is an urgent need to develop innovative methods. This study proposes a method of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data based on transfer learning. The method employs a multi-feature fusion approach to perform comprehensive quality evaluations without reference data, generating a sample database for data quality. Experimental results demonstrate that this method can provide quality composite scores and classification outcomes, offering technical support for the creation of the data quality sample library. Furthermore, an intelligent quality evaluation method based on transfer learning is used to conduct supervised quality assessments. Experimental results show that the transfer learning effects of ResNet50, VGG16, and GoogLeNet models are as expected. Among these, the ResNet50 and VGG16 models achieve higher accuracy in intelligent evaluations compared to the GoogLeNet model. Additionally, the classification results from the ResNet50 model exhibit the best consistency with the comprehensive evaluation results. The study concludes that the proposed method of autonomous quality evaluation for space remote sensing processing data based on transfer learning has promising applications in intelligent processing of space remote sensing data and provides an effective solution for practical applications.

title: Project-Based Learning as a Necessary Component in Training Industry 4.0 Professionals
authors: Olga Zvereva, Sergey Muslimov, Ekaterina Pirozhkova, Maksim Demchenko
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: This paper is to observe the experience of project-oriented learning in Institute of Civil Engineering and Architecture from UrFU. On the basis of a real student project discussion, techniques and instruments, that can help in project management and control, are observed. Successful project realization has proved that this kind activity can answer the appeal of Industry 4.0 to quick acquisition of new knowledge and skills in emerging cutting edge technologies. Without taking into account these new realities, one will lose in competitive struggle. By example of the project, where BIM technology has to be implemented, the effectiveness of information projects for students of non-information specialties has been shown, because during these projects implementation students acquire not only information technology application skills, but also explore the subject area in the most detail and deeply, receiving additional competencies in their professional field. The conclusions and recommendations outlined in the work are aimed at optimizing existing teaching methods for students of construction specialties under the Industry 4.0 conditions and can become the basis for developing new approaches to the organization of the educational process at all.

title: What Drives Consumers to Purchase in Live Streaming Marketplaces? A Systematic Review of Motivational Mechanisms
authors: Anisah Mutmainah, Reny Nadlifatin
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Live shopping has transformed the e-commerce landscape from static product displays to dynamic, interactive experiences. Despite its rapid growth, the literature regarding the influence of user motivation on purchase intention remains fragmented and context dependent. This study conducts a Systematic Literature Review following the PRISMA guidelines to synthesize current knowledge and identify the psychological mechanisms mediating consumer behavior. By rigorously filtering an initial pool of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2, 6 9 8}$</tex> records, this study analyzes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 2}$</tex> high-quality journal articles (Q1 and Q2) published between 2020 and 2025. The synthesis reveals that the primary drivers of purchase intention in live shopping have shifted from transactional factors, such as price and promotion, to socialinterpersonal elements, were streamer characteristics and platform interactivity act as dominant stimuli. Furthermore, psychological factors specifically cognitive and emotional trust, alongside engagement metrics like social presence and flow experience serve as critical mediators in converting user motivation into planned or impulsive purchasing actions. This review also identifies significant research gaps, including a strong geographical bias toward the Chinese market, a lack of exploration regarding situational moderator variables, and theoretical fragmentation between hedonic and utilitarian motivations. By mapping the landscape of purchase intention determinants, this study proposes an integrated understanding of the live shopping ecosystem and provides a human centered future research agenda.

title: An Intelligent DDoS Protection Architecture for Secure and Scalable Cloud Systems
authors: M. Balasubramani, S. Arivarasan, Arulamuthan R, Harish V, Jayakumar B, Logesh S
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Smart city services such as real-time traffic prediction and city analytics rely on DDoS protection to have secure and scalable cloud systems. As the latency of transportation and IoT applications is increasingly placed on cloud platforms, resilient network defense is becoming central. Current DDoS mitigation strategies rely on fixed thresholds, signature-based intrusion detection, or centralized filtering, which fail to respond to dynamic cloud traffic patterns. Such approaches have a high false-positive rate and do not generalize well to zero-day attacks that result from changes in smart city traffic patterns. The architecture is a proposal for a cloud-native DDoS protection framework that combines real-time traffic observation, evolutionary feature engineering, and hybrid machine learning. Learned XGBoost instances of known volumetric and protocollevel attack signatures, and a Long Short-Term Memory autoencoder instance learns normal spatio-temporal traffic behavior to detect anomalies and zero-day attacks. The architecture is implemented across cloud-edge models and core models to secure traffic prediction, connected vehicles, roadside units, and city control platforms. The system adjusts security enforcement to smart city dynamics by learning temporal correlations in the mobility-driven network traffic. The experimental results show high detection accuracy, a higher F1score, and lower response time under large-scale attack conditions. The findings validate increased scalability, strength, and foresight, which will facilitate secure traffic intelligence in the cloud for future smart city applications.

title: Early Detection of Brain Tumors from MRI Images using Machine Learning Models
authors: Bejin Benjamin B, Sherin Eliyas
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Brain tumors are serious neurological disorders that must be diagnosed quickly and accurately for patients to have better chances of survival and improved clinical outcomes. Because of the lack of side effects and the good detail/clarity offered by MRIs, MRIs are the primary way to identify brain tumors. However, MRIs must be interpreted by physicians, and any errors made by the physician require accurate interpretation. As a solution to these problems, this research proposes an automated way to determine the presence of a brain tumor to classify the different outcomes using MRI images. To achieve this, we will utilize advanced deep learning and machine learning technologies to implement a deep learning system. Four distinct groups are employed by the suggested strategy for organising brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans: meningioma, pituitary tumor, glioma, and no tumor. MRI images used in this study are obtained from the Kaggle Brain Tumor MRI dataset, along with other publicly available benchmark datasets. Prior to classification, methods for preparing images such as Normalisation and resizing, and noise reduction are applied to enhance image quality and improve model performance. Advanced deep learning models, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transfer learning architectures such as VGG16 and ResNet, are employed for feature extraction and classification. Additionally, Regular machine learning classifiers, such as RF (random forest) and supportive vector machine (SVM), are implemented for comparative analysis.
