title: MC-DT: Mechanism Compensation-Digital Twin Based Zero-Shot Diagnostic Scheme
authors: Zhongyu Gao, Aibin Yan, Yang Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: As the interaction layer between electronic devices and the external environment, ensuring the proper functioning of analog circuits (ACs) has garnered extensive attention from both academia and industry. Compound faults, a common type of fault in ACs, are challenging to handle with existing data-driven diagnostic approaches due to the difficulties in data collection. To address this issue, this paper proposes a zero-shot diagnostic framework based on mechanism compensation-digital twin, aiming to tackle the diagnostic challenge of compound faults in ACs under data scarcity conditions. Firstly, we introduce a mechanism compensation-digital twin-based data generation scheme (MC-DT). This scheme generates standard fault data for the target circuit through the constructed twin model. Building on this, we further propose a mechanism-fused sample generation scheme to compensate for the limitations inherent in twin model modeling. By employing the MC-DT, a multi-source homogeneous domain dataset can be generated for the target circuit. Secondly, to effectively bridge the distribution gap between the generated data and real-world data, we employ a multi-source domain generalization network to learn domain invariant representations from the generated data, thereby enhancing the model's performance in practical application scenarios. Through case studies conducted on existing ACs using a constructed test platform, the experimental results demonstrate that the proposed scheme achieves accuracies of 71.63%, 77.49%, and 74.64% in three different zero-shot diagnostic tasks, respectively, exhibiting significant advantages over advanced zero-shot methods.

title: Massive MIMO-DFRC System With 1-Bit ADCs/DACs
authors: Bowen Wang, Hongyu Li, Bin Liao, Ziyang Cheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) dual-functional radar-communication (DFRC) has emerged as a key enabler for next-generation aerospace, wireless, and electronic systems. To enhance energy efficiency and reduce hardware cost, we consider a hardware-friendly architecture, termed 1BitDFRC, which employs 1-bit digital-to-analog converters (DACs) at the DFRC transmitter and 1-bit analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) at the radar sensing receiver. Two joint transceiver designs are considered: 1): quality-of-service constrained scheme, and 2): quality-of-detection constrained scheme. These problems are non-trivial due to the 1-bit quantization, which induces continuous-to-discrete-value transformations and thus complicates both communication and radar sensing performance analysis. To gain some insights on the impact of such quantization on the performance of communication and radar sensing, we first characterize radar detection after 1-bit ADC quantization and analyze the communication bit error rate. Leveraging these insights, we define DFRC-oriented performance metrics that enable tractable reformulations of the design problems, which are efficiently solved via majorization-minimization and integer linear programming. The proposed analysis and design framework are extended to the multi-target case, showcasing the generality of the proposed methods. Numerical results validate the analysis and demonstrate that 1BitDFRC achieves an advantageous trade-off between sensing/communication performance and energy efficiency compared with DFRC equipped with only 1-bit DACs, only 1-bit ADCs, or ideal infinite-resolution transceivers.

title: Prediction of Articular Cartilage Markers with Multispectral Imaging
authors: Eloi Canals Pascual, Hamidreza Masjedi, Akuroma Tolvanen, Petri Paakkari, Miika Kiema, Arash Mirhashemi, Ervin Nippolainen, Johanna Laakkonen, Juha Töyräs, Isaac O. Afara
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a debilitating joint disease in which early microstructural and compositional changes in articular cartilage (AC) are challenging to detect with current diagnostic tools. We hypothesized that multispectral imaging (MSI) could capture optical signatures that predict key AC properties. To test this, we developed and evaluated an MSI-based approach for estimating tissue thickness, proteoglycan (PG) content, collagen fiber orientation, and cell morphological properties (area and circularity). Reflectance images of bovine patellar AC were acquired using a custom-built MSI system operating at six wavelengths (550–970 nm), and machine learning models were trained to predict the targeted markers. The models yielded reliable estimates for AC thickness, whereas for PG content, collagen fiber orientation, and cell circularity, they achieved moderate accuracy. This work suggests that MSI holds promise as a label-free tool for characterizing AC and detecting early degenerative changes.

title: Whole-Body Motion Planning With Active Visual Gaze Control for Mobile Manipulators
authors: Chenyu Zhang, Shiying Sun, Kuan Liu, Chuanbao Zhou, Xiaoguang Zhao, Min Tan, Yanlong Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Recent years have seen increased interest in the application of mobile manipulators in industrial and service sectors. However, current whole-body coordinated motion planning methods rely on prior maps and external sensors, making them susceptible to perception blind spots and limiting their applicability in complex, unknown environments. To address these challenges, we propose a whole-body motion planning method that integrates active gaze control. Specifically, our method employs information gain to adjust the gaze direction of camera gimbals actively, and combines this with efficient trajectory optimization and real-time replanning. In addition, a quadratic optimization is employed to simultaneously solve for whole-body motion and gaze velocity. This tight coupling between perception and planning significantly improves the safety and efficiency of navigation and operation in complex environments. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly improves real-time planning capabilities and perception performance of mobile manipulators in complex environments, providing an innovative solution for real-time manipulation.

title: A Novel Deep Learning Approach for Interferometric Phase Linking in Distributed Scatterer Interferometry
authors: Chunjing Chen, Shouyi Chen, Feng Xiang, Chisheng Wang, Chuanhua Zhu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Distributed Scatterer Interferometry (DSI) has emerged as a widely adopted InSAR processing strategy due to its ability to effectively enhance the phase quality of distributed scatterers (DS) while preserving spatial resolution. However, the phase linking (PL) step in DSI remains computationally intensive, posing challenges for large-scale applications. In this study, we propose a novel deep learning-based PL approach that effectively integrates spatiotemporal information from time-series interferograms to achieve rapid and robust phase optimization. The deep learning model was trained on real interferogram sequences and validated in low-coherence environments. These test regions included mixed areas of cropland, bare soil, and scattered buildings, a mine site, and grasslands. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves an average phase residue reduction of 95.98% and increases valid monitoring points to 179.44% of baseline values. Crucially, it requires only 2.04%-4.24% of the runtime and 22.58%-27.62% of the memory of conventional PL method. These improvements position our model as a computationally efficient and scalable tool, providing a practical approach for achieving enhanced phase quality in InSAR applications.

title: Hyperbolic Prototype Guidance for Incremental SAR ATR
authors: Yanjie Xu, Hao Sun, Chenfang Liu, Linbin Zhang, Kefeng Ji, Gangyao Kuang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Despite the success of deep learning methods in synthetic aperture radar automatic target recognition (SAR ATR), they face significant challenges in open environments. Deep models suffer from catastrophic forgetting when learning new classes. Moreover, SAR images are frequently degraded by serious noise, sidelobes, and clutter, necessitating robust representations. Existing methods typically employ Euclidean space optimization, which is not suitable for modeling SAR targets with hierarchical semantics. In contrast, hyperbolic space naturally captures hierarchical structures, demonstrating advantages in mitigating catastrophic forgetting and providing robust representation. In this article, we propose Hyperbolic Prototype Guidance (HPG) framework for incremental SAR ATR. First, HPG constructs a hierarchical topology in the adaptive hyperbolic embedding space. Specifically, the deep features of SAR images are projected onto a Poincaré ball and utilized to generate multi-level prototypes, thereby constructing a hierarchical topology. To accommodate the dynamic data distribution, complexity-aware curvature modulation provides a specific hyperbolic space tailored to the current topology. Secondly, HPG utilizes local sensitive prototype regularization to learn new classes, where the hierarchical topology undergoes local structural expansion to align with evolving SAR target taxonomies. Finally, HPG utilizes hierarchical prototype relation distillation to preserve the representation of old classes, where the global topological construction is maintained during incremental learning, alleviating catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on both class-incremental and data incremental learning across three benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of HPG, achieving state-of-the-art performance in incremental SAR ATR.

title: PROBE: Probabilistic Occupancy BEV Encoding with Analytical Translation Robustness for 3D Place Recognition
authors: Jinseop Lee, Byoungho Lee, Gichul Yoo
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: We present PROBE (PRobabilistic Occupancy BEV Encoding), a learning-free LiDAR place recognition descriptor that models each BEV cell's occupancy as a Bernoulli random variable. Rather than relying on discrete point-cloud perturbations, PROBE analytically marginalizes over continuous Cartesian translations via the polar Jacobian, yielding a distance-adaptive angular uncertainty $\sigma \_\theta = \sigma \_{t} / r$ in $\mathcal {O}(R{\cdot }S)$ time. The primary parameter $\sigma \_{t}$ represents the expected translational uncertainty in meters, a sensor-independent physical quantity that enhances cross-sensor generalization while reducing the need for extensive per-dataset tuning. Pairwise similarity combines a Bernoulli-KL Jaccard with exponential uncertainty gating and FFT-based height cosine similarity for rotation alignment. Evaluated on four datasets spanning four diverse LiDAR types, PROBE achieves the highest accuracy among handcrafted descriptors in multi-session evaluation and competitive single-session performance relative to both handcrafted and supervised baselines. The source code and supplementary materials are available at https://sites.google.com/view/probe-pr.

title: Human-Structure-Aware Token Position Embedding for Tokenized Pose Estimation_supp1-3700936.pdf
authors: Zhongqiu Zhao
publish: N/A
abstract: Tokenized pose estimation (TPE) has demonstrated remarkable performance in lightweight human pose estimation (HPE) models. However, existing TPE methods typically initialize keypoint tokens randomly, without explicitly incorporating human structure priors. These priors play a vital role in HPE by effectively mitigating common challenges such as occlusion and ambiguity. To this end, we propose a Structure-Aware Keypoint Position Embedding (SAKPE). This embedding explicitly encodes inherent structural properties of the human body, such as symmetry and order, into the positional coordinates of keypoint tokens. It also employs learnable scale and offset factors to adapt to diverse human poses, thereby fully exploiting the geometric constraints among keypoints. Furthermore, to better leverage the positional relationships among patch tokens, we introduce a Layer-adaptive Hybrid Patch Position Embedding (LHPPE). It dynamically fuses absolute and relative position embeddings of patch tokens based on attention distributions across Transformer layers, enabling the model to learn both absolute and relative positional information adaptively. Taking the two together, we propose a novel position embedding method for pose estimation, named Human-structure-aware Token Position Embedding (HTPE). It significantly improves the performance of various TPE models. Extensive experiments on COCO, CrowdPose, and OCHuman show that HTPE achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among lightweight methods, with a negligible increase in parameters and FLOPs. Notably, it demonstrates consistent improvements under occlusion, , achieving up to 3.3 AP gains. The source code can be found in https://github.com/guzejungithub/HTPE.

title: Spatially-Constrained Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
authors: Vitor Rosa Meireles Elias, John Dehls, Pierluigi Salvo Rossi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: This paper addresses the task of detecting spatiallyconstrained group anomalies, relevant in fields such as geosciences, environmental monitoring, and medical imaging. Classical approaches include scan statistics and local outlier factor, while modern methods are heavily based on autoencoder structures. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end machine learning (ML)- based unsupervised approach robust to abnormalities outside the anomalous region. We target geospatial data, where each point consists of a multivariate feature vector coupled with a coordinate vector. In this scenario, localized group anomalies are groups of points whose features do not adhere to some concept of normality across the dataset, and that are confined to a region within this dataset. Abnormalities outside this region are not considered part of the anomaly. The proposed methodology uses an ML-based clustering approach that solves a version of the graph mincut problem and the assumption that anomalous and non-anomalous data are clustered at different paces to generate an anomaly score for each point. With the networked graph representation, the proposed methodology can also be used when data points do not have absolute position vectors, but relative distances can be inferred from the datasets (e.g., in social networks a friendship relation indicates closeness between users and locality can be expressed as a group of friends). The proposed algorithm is compared using different metrics against a wide range of well-known state-of-the-art approaches for unsupervised anomaly detection, such as nearest-neighbors and autoencoderbased algorithms, outperforming them in most scenarios. We also provide an analysis of the algorithm applicability with respect to locality properties of the anomaly and the data structure.

title: Churnnet: An Intelligent Machine Learning Framework for Sustainable Customer Retention in Telecommunication Systems
authors: N. Varatharajan, P. Gowtham, M. Harish, C. Vinayaga Moorthy, S. Joel
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The churn of customers is also an acute problem in telecommunications systems because of the strong competition in the market and the emergence of substitutes for the service. Accurate churn prediction will help service providers develop viable customer retention plans and minimize revenue loss. Current churn-prediction models are often limited in their ability to capture feature interactions, are sensitive to class imbalance, and do not generalize well to other customer behavior patterns, leading to suboptimal retention decisions. This suggests an intelligent machine-learning system for sustainable customer retention called ChurnNet. ChurnNet combines high-order feature engineering, imbalance-sensitive learning, and a hybrid ensemble classification framework to model the sophisticated behavioral, service utilization, and billing trends. The framework comprises data normalization, correlation-based feature selection, and model training using the optimal method to improve predictive robustness and interpretability. On benchmark telecommunication datasets, it has been experimentally found that ChurnNet outperforms conventional machine learning models, with an accuracy of up to 89.5, a precision of 87.4 %, a recall of 86.2 %, an F1-score of 86.8 %, and can sustain excellent performance in highly imbalanced data conditions between the classes. The findings validate the assumption that ChurnNet is a powerful and scalable solution for proactive churn management, helping sustain customer retention and the use of data in decisionmaking in current telecommunication systems.

title: Modification of the XGBoost Machine Learning Algorithm for Sales Forecasting
authors: Yuri Leokhin, Timur Fatkhulin, Svetlana Dymkova
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This article examines approaches to regularization and mitigating the impact of data anomalies using the XGBoost algorithm as an example. The objective of the study is to develop and experimentally validate a modified XGBoost-based algorithm to improve the accuracy of sales forecasting. The relevance of this study lies in the fact that one of the key characteristics of the sales domain is the presence of noise and anomalies in the data, which, when forecasting, leads to a decrease in the accuracy of results and the occurrence of overfitting. Tuning existing parameters in gradient boosting algorithms does not always solve these problems and leads to the need for additional modifications to refine forecasts. This study proposes a gradient boosting modification method that uses adjustments to the gradient calculation using anomaly estimates for dataset samples based on the isolation forest method. Additional modifications to the proposed method are made through cross-validation. All modifications were tested in different combinations on different datasets. Thus, a pattern of increased accuracy during cross-validation training using the developed anomaly weighting method is revealed. The proposed method allows the model to be adapted to the characteristics of specific data and reduces the impact of common problems such as anomalies, noise, and overfitting.

title: Aegis-ASD: A Forensic Honeypot Framework for Impersonation Detection using ArcFace
authors: Saransh Singh, Bhupati Hemagirisai, Akshay Blahatia, Justina Michael J
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Biometric authentication is a key security measure for high security applications such as distributed identity networks and e-voting systems. However, current implementations use a strict pass/fail decision tree that triggers the alert to the attacker when failed, which results in repeated attempts to break in and physical confrontation with the terminal. Unlike previous deception-based systems, such as decoy credential stores or software honeypots, deployed at the network layer, the proposed framework employs deception in the biometric verification pipeline. Here, we present Aegis-ASD: a new Silent Biometric Honeypot framework with three primary contributions: (i) a high-precision ArcFace recognition engine that outputs 512-dimensional facial embeddings, (ii) an ASD (Absent, Shifted, Dead) threat intelligence module for identity cross-referencing, and (iii) an automated forensic quarantine mechanism. The system employs the deep learning model, ArcFace to extract a very discriminative 512-dimensional facial embedding to perform cosine similarity-based identity verification. Instead of merely denying spoofed attempts, the architecture integrates an ASD (Absent, Shifted, Dead) threat intelligence module to crosscheck the pre-classified database status of the claimed identity. The system silently activates a deception protocol when a biometric match successfully pairs with an inactive identity. It imitates successful frontend authentication, while the transaction is asynchronously quarantined by the backend and critical forensic evidence, including timestamps, biometric confidence scores, and attacker images in Base64 are logged. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach is effective in capturing impersonation attempts without confronting the attacker and offers a robust, forensic-aware security paradigm that significantly enhances threat intelligence capabilities in distributed networks.

title: Digital Transformation of Higher Education: Comparative Analysis of International EdTech Implementation Models and Their Economic Effectiveness
authors: Anna Glebova
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: The authors consider the global digital transformation of higher education through a comparative analysis of national EdTech implementation models in the United States, China, the European Union, and Russia. Drawing on empirical data from official statistics, government reports, and peer-reviewed literature, the paper evaluates the economic efficiency of each model using comparable indicators, including adoption scale, and cost-per-student reduction. The findings reveal divergent yet converging strategies: the US favors market-driven innovation, China employs a centrally coordinated scale-up, the EU emphasizes quality and inclusion through collaborative mechanisms, and Russia adopts a hybrid model combining state guidance and market dynamics. Evidence suggests that large-scale EdTech adoption can reduce delivery costs by up to 30% while expanding access. The paper concludes with recommendations for Russia, aligning international best practices with national conditions to optimize the effectiveness and efficiency of digital transformation in higher.

title: Time-Series Classification Using AI Models for Digital Twin Applications
authors: Afshin Eisazadeh Kharabeh, Victor Lawrence, Yu-Dong Yao
publish: 2026 35th Wireless and Optical Communications Conference (WOCC)
abstract: Digital Twin (DT) technology enables real-time monitoring and optimization through virtual replicas that stay synchronized with physical systems. This paper reviews AI-based time-series classification methods for DT applications, covering both classical machine learning and deep learning architectures, including CNNs, RNNs, TCNs, and Transformers. We discuss integration challenges such as real-time requirements, edge–cloud deployment, and multimodal data fusion, and highlight applications across manufacturing, healthcare, and infrastructure. Key challenges, including data scarcity, interpretability, and scalability, are identified as important directions for future research.

title: Integrating Digital Technologies and Sustainability in Logistics Monitoring Systems: A Systematic Review of Subsidized Commodity Distribution
authors: Muhammad Rizqy Abdurrahaman Assyifa, Elisa Kusrini, Agus Mansur, Muhammad Ridwan Andi Purnomo
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Digital transformation has reconfigured contemporary logistics systems and intensified the need for monitoring architectures capable of ensuring transparency, adaptive control, and governance integrity in complex and regulated supply environments. This study examines structural gaps in sustainability-oriented logistics monitoring within subsidized commodity distribution, focusing on the 3 kg LPG program, where allocation accuracy and accountability are critical due to its socio-economic relevance for lower-income communities. A structured bibliometric design integrating Systematic Literature Review (SLR), Systematic Literature Mapping (SLM), and VOSviewer-based co-word analysis was applied. From 685 Scopus-indexed publications (2018-2024), 23 Q1–Q3 studies were identified as the analytical corpus. The findings reveal that while sustainability represents a dominant thematic construct and demonstrates strong associations with digital technologies such as IoT, artificial intelligence, and blockchain, the literature largely treats these domains as parallel research streams rather than consolidating them into governance-oriented, real-time monitoring architectures suitable for regulated subsidy distribution systems. This indicates a structural fragmentation between sustainability discourse and operational monitoring design. To address this gap, the study synthesizes the mapped body of knowledge into a hierarchical conceptual framework that integrates logistics challenges, sustainability dimensions, technological enablers, analytical approaches, and system-level objectives. The contribution is conceptual and architectural, providing a structured synthesis that clarifies the configurational relationships between sustainability principles and digital monitoring design within regulated subsidy systems. The findings should therefore be interpreted as structural knowledge integration rather than empirical validation, establishing a disciplined foundation for future operational testing.

title: Smart Technology, Law, and Sustainable Development: Building Integrated Regulatory Frameworks for the Digital Era
authors: Qais Maaitah, Mohammed Khalid Buraa, Bader Ahmed Whaieb Taha, Marwah Imad Alghazali, Elsaka Maha, Khudr Bary Freeh Alsrray
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Smart technology constitutes one of the most significant structural transformations witnessed by the contemporary world. It has brought about a qualitative shift in patterns of production, management, and decision-making, and has become a fundamental pillar in the paths of economic, social, and environmental development. Applications of artificial intelligence, the Internet of Things, big data analytics, and advanced digital systems have reshaped the institutional structures of states, improved resource efficiency, and enhanced transparency and good governance. In light of the growing international interest in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), there has emerged a pressing need for an integrated legal framework that regulates the use of smart technologies and directs them toward achieving a balance between economic growth, social justice, and environmental protection. Although technology offers promising opportunities, it simultaneously raises complex legal challenges related to personal data protection, cybersecurity, civil liability for damages caused by intelligent systems, as well as issues concerning AI ethics and algorithmic discrimination. This research aims to analyze the legal role of smart technology in supporting and achieving sustainable development goals through three main axes. The first examines the theoretical foundations related to smart technology and sustainable development and clarifies the interrelationship between them. The second studies the legislative and regulatory frameworks governing the use of smart technologies at both national and international levels, and assesses their suitability in meeting sustainability requirements. The third analyzes contemporary challenges and future trends in light of rapid digital transformation, and proposes innovative legal mechanisms to enhance the ability of legislation to keep pace with technological developments. The significance of this study lies in demonstrating that law is no longer merely a traditional regulatory tool, but has become a strategic actor in directing technological innovation toward achieving sustainable development, ensuring the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms, and reinforcing principles of transparency, accountability, and digital justice. The study also seeks to present a set of legislative and institutional recommendations that contribute to developing a flexible legal system capable of adapting to rapid technological transformations.

title: Research Noise Immunity of Message Reception in Radio Engineering Systems
authors: A. H. Hasanov, T. T. Nadirova, B. G. Ibrahimov
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: Methods for improving the noise immunity of useful traffic message reception in radio engineering systems are analyzed. Based on this research, a new approach to constructing a mathematical model for assessing the noise immunity characteristics coherent modem reception in radio engineering systems operating under conditions of unintentional interference sources is proposed. The developed mathematical model takes into account the operating algorithm of the radio engineering system detector synthesis, decoding methods, and efficient modulation and coding methods. It is proposed to use the probability correct reception of a code message in radio communication as an indicator of noise immunity. Important analytical expressions are derived for assessing the noise immunity characteristics message reception, taking into account the receiver's spectral parameters.

title: Deep Learning Enhanced Detection of Multiple Sclerosis
authors: Gururaj K, Kesav Bala R, Lokesh G, Yazhini Nivethitha
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic nervous system disease which should be diagnosed promptly and correctly to be treated. This study outlines an automatic brain MRI-based deep learning approach to the detection of MS. Kaggle MS dataset comprises of the axial and sagittal FLAIR images whose data are processed and divided into training, validation, and testing groups. The convolutional neural network model is utilized to extract important features and classify binary. The proposed system had an average accuracy of 91.47% and it possesses high precision, recall and F1-score. The findings of the experiment show that the model is effective in the ability to differentiate between MS and healthy people, which justifies its application in clinical decision-making.

title: AI-Driven Anomaly Detection for Industrial Control Systems: A Comparative Study
authors: Dowson Liu, Xiaonan Song, Xue Li
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative study of AIdriven anomaly detection techniques for Industrial Control Systems (ICS), addressing the limitations of traditional rule-based intrusion detection. We evaluate three algorithms-Isolation Forest, Autoencoder, and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks-using two widely adopted ICS security datasets: Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) and Water Distribution (WADI). Performance is assessed through accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with attention to class imbalance where normal traffic dominates. Results show that deep learning approaches, particularly LSTM and Autoencoder models, outperform rule-based and tree-based methods by better capturing system dynamics and identifying subtle anomalies. These findings highlight the robustness of deep learning in real-world ICS deployments and contribute practical insights for strengthening critical infrastructure security.

title: Online Detection of Droplet Transfer Modes in CuCrZr Alloy WAAM Based on CNN-LSTM
authors: Tianxiang Shi, Zhaowei Diao, Fei Yang, Haichen Li, Lin Chen, Yi Wu, Mingzhe Rong
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This study presents an online detection approach for droplet transfer in CuCrZr alloy wire-arc additive manufacturing based on a CNN–LSTM hybrid network. The molten pool was dynamically monitored using a high-speed imaging system, and a dataset containing 46,641 labeled images of three typical transfer modes―liquid bridge, droplet, and unstable―were established. The proposed model integrates convolutional neural networks for spatial feature extraction and long short-term memory networks for temporal sequence modeling, enabling end-to-end recognition of dynamic transfer behaviors. Experimental validation shows that the CNN–LSTM model achieves a recognition accuracy of 96.67%, significantly outperforming conventional CNN, GRU, and BP-NN models. The results demonstrate that the method effectively captures spatiotemporal features and provides a reliable solution for real-time monitoring and intelligent control of droplet transfer in CuCrZr WAAM.

title: AI-Integrated Digital Twin Framework for Quality and Performance Management in Sustainable Buildings
authors: Mohammed Abdulrazzaq Alaghbari, Aawag Mohsen Alawag, Yasser El-Kassrawy, Mourad Mansour, Saleem Ahmed Al-Azazi, Basheer M. Al-Ghazali
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Operational quality in sustainable buildings is often compromised by fragmentation across Building Information Modeling (BIM), the Internet of Things (IoT), and Building Management Systems (BMS). This paper proposes an Artificial Intelligence (AI)-integrated Semantic Digital Twin (SDT) framework that redefines quality management as a traceable and governance-ready operational process. The framework combines ontology-driven semantics, hybrid physics-AI modeling, and a requirements-to-KPI traceability graph to connect lifecycle intent, data provenance, analytic outputs, and operator decisions. A seven-layer architecture is introduced together with an assurance loop that validates data, quantifies uncertainty, records explainability artifacts, and enforces human approval before controlled actuation. To address evaluation needs, the paper also defines a benchmark-ready Design Science Research blueprint using BOPTEST and governance metrics, including energy use intensity, comfort violation hours, CO2 exceedance hours, fault detection performance, uncertainty calibration, traceability completeness, and approval latency. The contribution is therefore not only an architectural model, but also a practical audit-ready basis for trustworthy and scalable building operations.

title: Water-Content-Aware Machine Learning for Screening Multiphysics Tissue Property Tables
authors: Fatima AlZaabi, Ali Al Bataineh
publish: IEEE Pulse
abstract: Tissue property tables underpin radio frequency (RF) safety assessments, bioheat simulations, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) specific absorption rate (SAR) modelling. Because such tables consolidate measurements from many independent studies spanning different protocols and tissue conditions, cross-property patterns can be subtle and difficult to verify manually. We present a water-content-aware screening workflow that learns these cross-property relationships from the IT’IS Tissue Properties Database V5.0 (112 entries) and uses them to produce a ranked check-first list of entries that merit closer review. Three methodological contributions distinguish this work: (i) a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100 \%$ </tex-math></inline-formula>-missing feature column is explicitly identified and removed before modelling; (ii) binary missingness indicator features are added alongside median imputation, enabling the model to treat observed and imputed values differently; and (iii) a systematic duplicate featurevector audit is conducted before fitting, revealing 16 entry pairs that share identical post-imputation profiles. An Extremely Randomized Trees regressor achieves 5-fold cross-validation <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{R}^2=0.630$ </tex-math></inline-formula> (MAE <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$=7.48$ </tex-math></inline-formula> percentage points (pp), RMSE = 17.04 pp). Split conformal prediction wraps the model with distribution-free <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$90 \%$ </tex-math></inline-formula> uncertainty bands (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\hat{\mathrm{q}}=14.0 \mathrm{pp}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>). The resulting screening list and uncertainty estimates give simulation teams a principled, automated starting point for traceability review before tissue properties enter multi-physics workflows.

title: Design Features and Practical Implementation of Class-AB Differential Stages in High-Speed Gallium Arsenide Operational Amplifiers
authors: Ilya V. Pakhomov, Dmitriy V. Kuznetsov, Dmitriy V. Kleimenkin, Nikolay N. Prokopenko
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: A novel differential cascade circuit has been developed and investigated, implemented using a GaAs technology process that allows the use of nJFET and p-n-p bipolar transistors. Computer modeling of the proposed circuit solutions has been performed, incorporating nonlinear correction circuits for the transfer characteristics of the differential cascade, which increase the maximum slew rate (SR) of the operational amplifier (Op-Amp) by more than an order of magnitude. The simultaneous effect of nonlinear correction circuits and a transient-response differentiating circuit has been analyzed, enabling a further increase in SR (up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4235 ~\mathrm{V} / \mu \mathrm{s}$</tex>). This paper is dedicated to the study of one of the most in-demand analog IP modules for embedded systems - a GaAs operational amplifier which, thanks to new circuit design solutions in the input stage, provides enhanced maximum slew rate values while maintaining low quiescent current and, consequently, reduced self-heating of the input transistors.

title: RIS-Assisted UAV Networks with Energy Harvesting and Data Collection
authors: Tassnim Mohamed, Liang Zhang
publish: 2026 35th Wireless and Optical Communications Conference (WOCC)
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are widely used in wireless-powered communication networks (WPCNs) to deliver energy and collect data from distributed Internet of Things (IoT) sensors. However, conventional Harvest-Then-Transmit (HTT) protocols may suffer from limited efficiency when multiple sensors share the same time frame for communication. In this paper, we propose a mini-frame communication scheme assisted by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) to improve energy transfer and uplink data transmission in UAV-enabled WPCNs. A low-complexity heuristic algorithm is developed to determine the optimal time-splitting factor, align RIS phases to strengthen wireless channels, and allocate uplink transmission time among sensors using a fairness-based rule. Simulation results show that the proposed mini-frame and RIS-assisted approach achieves higher sum throughput than conventional HTT-based schemes and maintains consistent performance gains across different numbers of sensors, RIS elements, and UAV transmission power levels.

title: A Systematic Review of AI in Leadership, Sales, and Management: Techniques, Use Cases, and Future Directions
authors: M Sharmila, Prasanth N, Arjun, V Sumalatha
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the modern business landscape by enhancing the leadership approach, sales performance, and altering the way organizations are managed. Machine learning, predictive analytics, natural language processing, and intelligent automation are being integrated into business systems, which are enabling data-driven decision-making, engaging customers uniquely, and adaptive enterprise governance. This review paper is a systematic review of AI uses in three key business areas, namely leadership intelligence, sales, and managerial decision support. It discusses basic AI processes, including supervised learning, deep learning, reinforcement learning, and hybrid intelligence structures, and reveals the impacts that they have on the process of strategic planning, revenue forecasting, workforce optimization, and customer relationship management. The research also determines gaps in research related to explainability, ethical governance, complexity, and scalability of the integration in the enterprise adoption. In the synthesis of literature about the present and constantly growing trends in the field of technology, this review provides extensive content to comprehend how the concept of AI is transforming the role of business leadership, sales optimization, and management ecosystems under the conditions of digital transformation.

title: Intelligent Scheduling of Sensor Nodes Using Machine Learning Techniques in Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Saleem H Majeed, Khattab M. Ali Alheeti, Sarah Amjad Inad, Salah Sleibi Al-Rawi, Ameen R. Jihad Alalwanl
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) are devices that monitor physical and environmental phenomena and collect data from the surrounding environment and transmit it to the base station. WSNs have demonstrated their excellent capabilities in a variety of industries. However, sensor nodes have many limitations, such as limited battery capacity and communication capabilities. Scheduling the work of sensor nodes is essential to help conserve battery consumption and improve connectivity. This study aims to find a scheduling algorithm for sensor nodes using machine learning; an intelligent system is proposed to schedule the node in four modes automatically: active mode, safe mode, Hard-safe mode, and sleep mode, depending on the remaining power to hold the sensor. The Simulation Network-2 simulator is used to generate the data set and calculate the residual energy of nodes, and the Distance on-demand vector routing (AODV) was used as a routing protocol. Running the simulation and classification using the support vector machines algorithm proposed in this study showed the best accuracy and error rate of 100 % and 0.0 %, respectively, compared to other machine learning algorithms.

title: Local Minima of Error Function in Neural Networks
authors: Victor N. Tolstykh, Vladimir N. Yershov
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: The well-known problem of neural network training algorithms getting stuck (jammed) in the vicinity of points called “local minima” is discussed. Arguments are presented to support the assertion that local minima are virtually nonexistent, and the jamming occurs near critical points with a signature different from that of local minima. The shortcomings of known optimization methods, which slowdown algorithm of gradient descent performance, are demonstrated. An algorithm for bypassing critical points using directed manipulation of stochastic descent is proposed, partially supported by simulations using model data. The presented approach can significantly accelerate neural network training and improve the quality of the neural network itself by achieving an optimal distribution of neural network weights.

title: GridHarmony: Hybrid Metaheuristic-Driven Secondary Distributed Control for Stable and Optimal Power Sharing in DC Microgrids
authors: Kamalkumar T, S. Gomathi
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The progressive amalgamation of renewable sources of energy like photovoltaic facilities, battery storage facilities, and electric vehicle charging stations has greatly modified the working dynamics of contemporary DC microgrids. Nevertheless, renewable intermittency, communication delays and dynamic load variations tend to cause voltage instability, unequal current sharing and performance degradation of such systems. The traditional secondary control techniques of droop control or proportional-integral (PI) controllers cannot ensure a stable voltage recovery and optimal power allocation in extremely dynamic environments. To solve these problems, the current paper suggests a hybrid metaheuristic-based secondary distributed control system called GridHarmony, which aims to improve voltage stability and the optimal power sharing in DC microgrids. The new system combines a Bi-Directional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network to predict short-term disturbances with a hybrid optimization algorithm that consists of Improved Grey Wolf Optimizer (IGWO), Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO), and Differential Evolution (DE). These algorithms are working together to optimize the parameters of the controllers and enhance better convergence. In addition, the distributed consensus-based control mechanism allows autonomous co-ordination between converters without centralized control. The results obtained in simulations show that the framework suggested can effectively enhance the accuracy of the voltage restoration, the parasitic sharing error, power loss and system stability as a whole, unlike the traditional control methods. The proposed GridHarmony framework contributes to SDG 7 (Affordable and Clean Energy), SDG 9 (Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production), and SDG 13 (Climate Action) by enabling intelligent distributed control, efficient renewable energy integration, stable power sharing, and sustainable DC microgrid operation.

title: Optimized FPGA Architecture for DCT-based Harmonic Wavelet Transform with Reduced Latency and Power
authors: A M V Pathi, M Sai Mukhesh, M Utkarsh Nandan, M Dileep Kumar, K M V S Subrahmanyam
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The Harmonic wavelet transforms (HWT) is essential for multiresolution signal and image analysis. The Discrete Cosine transform (DCT) is the technique used for its strong energy compaction and computational complexity. This research work presents the optimized FPGA implementation of harmonic wavelet transform using DCT. The proposed technique highlights the optimized resource allocation, parallel pipelined architecture with efficient multiplier design. The DCT architecture implemented using parallel processing units which is followed by the harmonic wavelet transform. The approximated multiplier in the proposed architecture further reduces the complexity of the overall design. The proposed design is implemented on FPGA which shows the reduction in latency and power. The optimized design well suited for real time image processing and signal processing applications.

title: AI-Driven Smart EVM using GAN and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Bonthala Venkata Ranga Sai Teja, S Suguna Mallika
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The Electronic voting systems are an integral part of democratic processes, but in the existing Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs), manual or ID verification methods dominate, making them susceptible to impersonation, proxy voting, and manual errors. To mitigate such issues, the following paper will introduce an intelligent multi-stage voting verification process using computer vision, deep learning, and decision intelligence algorithms in electronic voting systems. The system is capable of carrying out person detection, mask detection, as well as facial recognition based on a pre-registered voter database to enable only an actual, valid, and mask-wearing-off voter to engage in the voting process. To enhance the reliability of facial feature recognition in low-lighting conditions, blurred images, as well as images affected by camera noises, a facial image enhancement module based on a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) is used before the extraction of facial features. Additionally, a Reinforcement Learning (RL) agent optimizes authentication thresholds based on analysis of the authenticity scores, as well as repeated attempts at capturing images. After successfully authenticating, the system checks for voting eligibility before granting permission to cast their votes. The modern experimental analysis proves that the proposed solution strengthens the authentication process, minimizes impersonation, and maximizes efficiency in EVM compared to traditional systems. The new architecture provides an intelligent scale-up solution for the next-generation secure electronic voting system. The proposed system yields an overall authentication accuracy of above 95%, establishing the proposed approach to be quite trustworthy for real-time secure voting applications, and therefore superior compared to traditional approaches of biometric voting.

title:
Room-temperature Ferromagnetism and Magnetocaloric effect in Intermetallic compound TbNi
                    <sub>1.5</sub>
                    Fe
                    <sub>0.5</sub>
                    : Bulk magnetization and Neutron diffraction studies
authors: Mitali Madhusmita Prusty, K. N. R. Tejaswi, J. Arout Chelvane, S. Rayaprol, A. V. Morozkin, R. Nirmala
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Rare-earth intermetallic compound TbNi<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1.5</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sub> (cubic, MgCu<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-type structure) has been prepared by arc-melting and characterized by dc magnetization measurements. Magnetization data reveal a ferromagnetic transition at 323 K (TC). Neutron diffraction study performed at 300 K also suggests a ferromagnetic state. While this transition could be ascribed to the ordering of iron moments, the magnetization gradually increases and saturates only at low temperatures indicating the order of the terbium moments. The sample has also been prepared by melt-spinning technique. The melt-spun TbNi<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1.5</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sub> shows a fine-grain microstructure and this sample also orders ferromagnetically at 319 K. Magnetization grows gradually as a function of temperature below TC, saturating below about 25 K and this behavior is witnessed in the magnetocaloric effect as well. Thus, partial substitution of Fe in the Ni site of TbNi<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> has resulted in increase of TC from about 37 K to near room temperature and leads to a flat, table-like magnetocaloric effect in the TbNi<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1.5</sub>Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sub> compound.

title: CURA: Size Isn’t All You Need – A Compact Underlying Architecture for On-Device Intelligence
authors: Jae-Bum Seo, Muhammad Salman, Lismer Andres Caceres-Najarro
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Existing on-device AI architectures for resource-constrained environments face two critical limitations: they lack compactness, with parameter requirements scaling proportionally to task complexity, and they exhibit poor generalizability, performing effectively only on specific application domains (e.g., models designed for regression tasks cannot adapt to natural language processing (NLP) applications). In this paper, we propose CURA, an architecture inspired by analog audio signal processing circuit that provides a compact and lightweight solution for diverse machine learning tasks across multiple domains. Our architecture offers three key advantages over existing approaches: (1) Compactness: it requires significantly fewer parameters regardless of task complexity; (2) Generalizability: it adapts seamlessly across regression, classification, complex NLP and computer vision tasks; and (3) Complex pattern recognition: it can capture intricate data patterns while maintaining extremely low model complexity. We evaluated CURA across diverse datasets and domains. For compactness, it achieved equivalent accuracy using up to 2,500 times fewer parameters compared to baseline models. For generalizability, it demonstrated consistent performance across four NLP benchmarks and one computer vision dataset, nearly matching specialized existing models (achieving F1-scores up to 90%). Lastly, it delivers superior forecasting accuracy for complex patterns, achieving 1.6 times lower mean absolute error and 2.1 times lower mean squared error than competing models.

title: Causal Prior–Driven Visual Grounding for Remote Sensing
authors: Yuhan Wang, Lingling Li, Xu Liu, Licheng Jiao, Xiaoqiang Lu, Fang Liu, Wenping Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote-sensing visual localization aims to accurately identify target regions from natural-language descriptions under complex imaging conditions. Although recent multimodal approaches have achieved notable progress via text-guided attention mechanisms, their decision processes often treat text-induced correlations as the primary source of evidence, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shifts. From a causal perspective, visual grounding can be viewed as a perception–decision process influenced by latent causal factors. Specifically, image observations are jointly affected by stable structural causal factors and external confounders, among which the former serve as the primary source of reliable and transferable evidence for prediction. Motivated by this insight, we propose a causal prior–driven visual grounding (CPVG) framework that explicitly models and exploits structural causal priors in visual data. CPVG instantiates causal prior–conditioned query modeling (CPCQM) to build a dual-query mechanism where structural causal priors and textual information cooperate, enabling priors to directly participate in query generation and visual evidence retrieval. Furthermore, to assess whether the performance gains brought by prior-guided queries are associated with more causally relevant information, we propose causal evidence attribution learning (CEAL), which introduces controlled counterfactual comparisons to encourage the model to rely more on stable structural evidence. Experiments conducted on the DIOR-RSVG and OPT-RSVG benchmarks demonstrate that CPVG yields consistent improvements in both localization accuracy and robustness, validating the effectiveness of combining distributed causal-prior modeling with causal evidence attribution learning.

title: Full-Trajectory Reconstruction for UHR-SAR Large-Scene Imaging via Joint Residual Motion Errors and Dominant Target Positions Estimation
authors: Shen Zhong, Yu Hai, Zhongyu Li, Junjie Wu, Ruoming Li, Jianyu Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Advances in imaging applications require radars to achieve higher resolution and cover larger scenes. Accurate full-trajectory information is crucial for obtaining well-focused large-scene ultrahigh-resolution (UHR) synthetic aperture radar images with centimeter-level resolution. However, the positioning accuracy of most INS/GPS, even high-end models such as the POS AV 610 RTX, is inadequate to meet such imaging requirements, thus necessitating full-trajectory reconstruction. This process involves two major challenges. (1) In UHR imaging, residual motion errors induce nonsystematic range cell migration that exceeds half a range cell, causing severe 2D defocusing. This defocusing not only hinders accurate dominant target position determination but also complicates the estimation of azimuth phase errors. (2) In large-scene imaging, the full-trajectory spans multiple full apertures, but the information provided by any dominant target is confined to one aperture, making direct full-trajectory reconstruction impossible. To overcome these challenges, this paper improves the conventional range history model through a first-order Taylor expansion with respect to the residual motion errors and dominant target position errors, and formulates a range history error minimization problem. To efficiently solve the resulting complex high-dimensional optimization, a dimensionality reduction strategy is developed, which enables joint estimation of the residual motion errors and the dominant target positions. Combined with trajectory fusion, the proposed method reconstructs a full-trajectory capable of covering the entire large scene. Simulations and experimental results demonstrate that the reconstructed trajectory provides sufficient accuracy to support well-focused UHR large-scene imaging.

title: Evaluating Image Forgery Detection and Propaganda in Manipulated Images of the Russian Trolls Campaign_supp1-3702065.pdf
authors: Yusuf Mucahit Cetinkaya
publish: N/A
abstract: Generative AI tools have increased the scale and accessibility of deceptive image creation, but the robustness of existing forgery detection methods under emerging editing tools and real-world social media conditions remains uncertain. This study evaluates state-of-the-art image forgery detectors on recent benchmarks and applies the strongest model to a historical influence-campaign case study: Russian troll activity during the 2016 U.S. presidential election. We find that TruFor is the most consistent detector across legacy and recent datasets, but that its performance degrades on edits produced by advanced tools such as Nano Banana. We then deploy a multimodal pipeline combining forgery localization, Optical Character Recognition (OCR), face recognition, and visual-language-model-based annotation to characterize manipulated images in the campaign. Our analysis suggests that Russian troll accounts relied primarily on low-effort meme-style edits and ridicule-oriented reputation attacks, disproportionately targeting prominent U.S. political figures including Hillary Clinton, Bill Clinton, and Barack Obama.

title: Dual-Resonant Electromagnetic Reshaping for Wireless Charging of Drones
authors: Zhen Zhang, Yizhuang Li, Yantian Gong, Yu Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes a control-oriented magnetic field shaping scheme rather than adopting complex coil topology, which can establish a fully coverage wireless charging area for drones. The position bias between the charging pad and drones is unavoidable due to environmental conditions, flight controllers, and drivers, which significantly deteriorates the charging performance. Accordingly, this article proposes a dual-resonant-based reshaping (dREMagR) scheme for wireless charging systems, where the magnetic field of different frequencies are artfully combined and mutually compensated to establish a fully-coverage energizing range for drones. Additionally, the multiple-frequency resonating compensating network is adopted to avoid the impact of cross-coupling effect between adjacent coils on the performance of power transmission. Lastly, simulation and experiment verification are both carried out in this article, which illustrates that the proposed dREMagR scheme can ensure that drones to capture the charging power within 9.23% of fluctuation at any position with any angle.

title: Design and Evaluation of a Dual-HSA Compliant Wrist Module for Prosthetic Applications_supp1-3691678.mp4
authors: Giovanni Berselli
publish: N/A
abstract: Conventional rigid prosthetic wrists often provide limited degrees of freedom and poor passive adaptability under external loading. To address this limitation, this work proposes a flexible prosthetic wrist based on dual handed shearing auxetic (HSA) cylinders for active flexion/extension (F/E) and pronation/supination (P/S). A wrist-oriented HSA unit design is introduced by combining a transformed lever pattern with a tailored lever angle to improve bidirectional load capability within a compact dual-cylinder architecture. A combined geometric/mechanical analysis and finite-element simulation framework is then established to investigate the influence of the main design variables on wrist functionality and performance. Based on this process, an optimized design is selected, fabricated via mold-casting, and validated experimentally using a tailored test platform integrating servo actuation, IMU sensing, and load-cell measurement. The prototype achieves approximately <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$49.6^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> F/E and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$72.3^\circ$</tex-math></inline-formula> P/S motion range, about <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.1\text{-}\mathrm{N\rm{\cdot }m}$</tex-math></inline-formula> drive torque, and over <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.8\text{-}\mathrm{N\rm{\cdot }m}$</tex-math></inline-formula> load torque, while preserving near-linear torque–angle behavior (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> up to 0.99). An IMU-based proportional–integral–differential closed-loop test demonstrates the stabilization of the output-cap posture under distal loading. These results support the feasibility of handed-shearing-auxetic-based compact compliant wrist modules for prosthetic applications.

title: Impulsive-Noise-Aware EP Detector for Massive MIMO under a Bernoulli-Gaussian Model
authors: Hua Li, Jiaqi Feng, Yuqing Feng, Zhenyue Huang, Shiya Hao, Qianqian Li, Xiaoming Dai
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Expectation propagation (EP) provides an attractive complexity-performance tradeoff for uplink massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) detection. However, its standard formulation relies on the additive white Gaussian noise assumption and is therefore vulnerable to impulsive, non-Gaussian disturbances. This work proposes an impulsive-noise-aware EP (INA-EP) detector for massive MIMO systems under Bernoulli-Gaussian (BG) impulsive noise. At each iteration, INA-EP performs scalar BG inference on the receive-antenna residuals to obtain posterior statistics of the impulsive component. These statistics are then incorporated into the EP update through an impulse-mitigated effective observation and the corresponding effective noise variance, thereby reducing the impact of sparse large-amplitude outliers. Simulation results demonstrate that INA-EP consistently outperforms conventional EP in both impulsive-noise estimation mean squared error (MSE) and uncoded bit error rate (BER), with larger gains at higher impulsive activity, while remaining robust under imperfect channel state information.

title: Photonic Spiking Graph Neural Network for Energy-Efficient Structured Data Processing
authors: Wanting Yu, Shuiying Xiang, Xingxing Guo, Shangxuan Shi, Haowen Zhao, Xintao Zeng, Yahui Zhang, Hongbo Jiang, Yue Hao
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Photonic computing exhibits broad application potential in signal processing and artificial intelligence (AI) acceleration owing to its high computational speed, low energy consumption, and intrinsic parallelism. Existing photonic computing research has predominantly concentrated on convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and fully connected neural networks (FCNNs), which are typically applied to tasks such as image classification and object detection. However, these traditional network architectures face inherent limitations in effectively processing graph-structured data. Graph neural networks (GNNs), specifically designed for modeling and analyzing graph-structured data, effectively capture the complex relationships among data points. We propose an computational architecture termed the photonic spiking graph neural network (PSGNN). The proposed architecture integrates the efficient structural modeling capability of GNNs, the temporal dynamics of spiking neurons, and the ultra-high speed and low power consumption advantages of photonic chips for parallel computation. Through hardware–software co-optimization, a bias-term simulation mechanism tailed for photonic computing chip is implemented via a feature-dimension expansion strategy, thereby facilitating effective model training. Experimental results on the KarateClub (PubMed) datasets demonstrate a training accuracy of 100% (92 ± 2%) and a test accuracy of 97% (90 ± 1%). A hardware experimental platform based on a silicon-photonics-based 4 × 4 Mach–Zehnder interferometer (MZI) array was constructed to perform classification experiments on the KarateClub datasets, achieving a test accuracy of 93%, thereby validating the effectiveness of the proposed architecture in structured-data processing tasks. The proposed system achieved an inference latency of 97 ps, demonstrating remarkable acceleration within the optical domain. The photonic linear computation achieved an energy efficiency of 280 GOPS/W and a computational density of 52 GOPS/mm². These results underscore the potential of PSGNN for deployment across multiple domains, including dynamic topology analysis of social networks, intelligent traffic scheduling and early-warning systems, and fault diagnosis in industrial IoT.

title: Taming Generative Synthetic Data for X-ray Prohibited Item Detection
authors: Jialong Sun, Hongguang Zhu, Weizhe Liu, Yunda Sun, Renshuai Tao, Yunchao Wei
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Training prohibited item detection models requires a large amount of X-ray security images, but collecting and annotating these images is time-consuming and laborious. To address data insufficiency, X-ray security image synthesis methods composite images to scale up datasets. However, previous methods primarily follow a two-stage pipeline, where they implement labor-intensive foreground extraction in the first stage and then composite images in the second stage. Such a pipeline introduces inevitable extra labor cost and is not efficient. In this paper, we propose a one-stage X-ray security image synthesis pipeline (Xsyn) based on text-to-image generation, which incorporates two effective strategies to improve the usability of synthetic images. The Cross-Attention Refinement (CAR) strategy leverages the cross-attention map from the diffusion model to refine the bounding box annotation. The Background Occlusion Modeling (BOM) strategy explicitly models background occlusion in the latent space to enhance imaging complexity. To the best of our knowledge, compared with previous methods, Xsyn is the first to achieve high-quality X-ray security image synthesis without extra labor cost. Experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms all previous methods with 1.2% mAP improvement, and the synthetic images generated by our method are beneficial to improve prohibited item detection performance across various X-ray security datasets and detectors. Code is available at https://github.com/pILLOW-1/Xsyn/.

title: NDPool: Correctness-Preserving Shared Execution for Efficient LLM Inference on CXL-NDP Systems
authors: Yeji Jung, Hwanjun Lee, Sungju Kim, Seulki Kim, Yunhyeong Jeon, Daehoon Kim
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: The inference performance of Large Language Models (LLMs) is increasingly constrained by memory capacity and bandwidth rather than raw computation, particularly for long-context workloads. CXL-based Near-Data Processing (CXL-NDP) has emerged as a promising solution for expanding memory capacity, but existing systems suffer from severe resource under-utilization due to exclusive device allocation under hybrid parallel execution. To minimize per-request latency, prior designs dedicate multiple NDP devices to tensor parallelism for FC layers, while non-FC layers are executed only on a single master device. As a result, most auxiliary devices remain idle for a large fraction of execution time, fundamentally limiting overall system throughput. Safe device sharing is challenging because out-of-order peer-to-peer communication can break request isolation and computational correctness at the shared CXL receive path. To address this limitation, we propose <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">NDPool</monospace>, a correctness-preserving shared execution framework that dynamically reuses idle TP devices across concurrent requests for FC-layer execution. <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">NDPool</monospace> combines ordered receive control for safe data delivery with adaptive scheduling to reduce execution bubbles and maximize shared-device utilization. Experimental results show that, under equivalent service-level objective (SLO) constraints, <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">NDPool</monospace> improves system throughput by up to 3.7× and by 1.5× on a geometric mean basis over the state-of-the-art CXL-NDP hybrid parallel design.
