title: Loss-Centric Cyber Resilience Evaluation and Automated Countermeasure Ranking Using Public Network Flow Data
authors: Hyunwook You, Jisoo Jang, Minjin Choi, Jina Lee, Haneul Ryu, Dongkyoo Shin
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Cyber resilience is a critical capability for minimizing damage upon a security breach and rapidly restoring system operations. However, quantitatively evaluating resilience and automating top-ranked countermeasure selection under the stated modeling assumptions remain challenging in public network flow data environments that lack detailed asset identification information. This paper demonstrates the feasibility of a resilience evaluation framework that quantifies attack tolerance from a system loss perspective by combining an internal communication graph constructed from benign traffic with threat-specific vulnerability severity. The proposed model computes asset importance from the structural centrality of the network and attack exposure from unique inbound connectivity independent of importance, while defining multi-hop neighbor sets centered on target assets as the scenario scope to ensure evaluation consistency. In particular, countermeasure candidates derived from systematic attack-defense mappings are modeled not as simple probability adjustments but as graph attribute manipulation operations that directly alter network properties, thereby clearly simulating how effectively each defensive measure reduces system loss through pre- and post-application loss differences. Furthermore, by measuring the end-to-end pipeline execution time from countermeasure candidate generation through loss recalculation to top-ranked countermeasure selection, the framework demonstrates a substantial reduction in decision latency compared to manual analysis. As a proof of concept validated on a single public dataset, the proposed framework provides a reproducible resilience automation methodology that jointly addresses countermeasure effectiveness comparison and decision-time measurement under constrained-observability conditions.

title: From Ops to Dev: A Dual-Layer Programmable Data Lake with Kubernetes and WebAssembly-based Offloading
authors: Ho Kim, Jongwon Kim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a dual-layer approach that evolves the data lake from a passive repository into a platform that developers can directly program. Although modern object stores expose limited server-side operations, control typically resides with infrastructure operators, preventing data consumers, such as data scientists, from leveraging computing resources near data. This results in repeated inefficiency when large datasets are shipped across a network for processing. To address this, we design aWebAssembly-based ‘‘Connected Data Lake’’ framework that enables data consumers to deploy and execute storage applications in aWebAssembly sandbox within storage nodes, so that custom APIs can be offloaded onto storage nodes. Using WebAssembly’s portability, we abstract and integrate Linux’s io_uring-based I/O and offloadable operations to be invoked on storage nodes, delivering high-performance, low-resource I/O on general-purpose hardware. We evaluated our prototype in two complementary experiments. First, in a log filtering scenario using approximately 29.61 GiB of raw data, the proposed data operation offloading reduced the network transfer volume by 99.978% and achieved a 45.5% faster end-to-end processing time compared to the conventional compute-pulls-data model, even with WebAssembly sandbox overhead. Second, in an S3-compatible object I/O benchmark against a conventional object store, our prototype improved GET throughput by 10.1–22.2% for small-to-medium objects, PUT throughput by up to 438.6% for small objects, and STAT throughput by 15.9%, while consistently reducing latency across all PUT percentiles for objects up to 1MB and lowering CPU per operation by 24–86% for GET and 35–77% for PUT, with a 4.0–6.2×smaller memory footprint. These results show that data consumers can safely program object storage and that the architecture provides a pragmatic path to data lake implementations.

title: MSDiff: Multi-Strategy Diffusion Model for Social Behavior Modeling and Recommendation
authors: Jiawei Wang, Changhe Liu, Yuan Gao, Laurence T. Yang, Xiaotong Zhou, Jing Yang, Sai Gu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Understanding and simulating user behavior in social networks is fundamental to computational social intelligence. Traditional recommendation methods rely on discriminative models that predict user preferences but fail to capture the generative processes underlying social influence propagation and behavior formation. In this article, we propose multi-strategy diffusion (MSDiff), a MSDiff model that leverages generative AI to model, simulate, and enhance social user behavior for recommendation. Our approach introduces three key innovations: 1) a selective diffusion mechanism that specifically activates dormant users by simulating latent social influence propagation, effectively modeling behavior emergence in inactive populations; 2) a differential generative strategy that treats user-user (U-U) and user-item (U-I) interactions distinctly—using U-U diffusion to simulate social homophily dynamics and U-I diffusion to generate contrastive behavioral samples; and 3) a collaborative graph convolution layer that integrates multirelational signals to model complex social dynamics. By framing social recommendation as a generative modeling problem, MSDiff not only improves recommendation accuracy but also provides interpretable simulations of how user preferences form and propagate through social networks. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that MSDiff significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness in both behavior simulation and recommendation tasks.

title: Token Expand-Merge: Training-Free Token Compression for Vision-Language-Action Models
authors: Yifan Ye, Jiaqi Ma, Jun Cen, Zhihe Lu
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pretrained on large-scale multimodal datasets have emerged as powerful foundations for robotic perception and control. However, their massive scale, often billions of parameters, poses significant challenges for real-time deployment, as inference becomes computationally expensive and latency-sensitive in dynamic environments. To address this, we propose Token Expand-and-Merge-VLA (TEAM-VLA), a training-free token compression framework that accelerates VLA inference while preserving task performance. TEAM-VLA introduces a dynamic token expansion mechanism that identifies and samples additional informative tokens in the spatial vicinity of attention-highlighted regions, enhancing contextual completeness. These expanded tokens are then selectively merged in deeper layers under action-aware guidance, effectively reducing redundancy while maintaining semantic coherence. By coupling expansion and merging within a single feed-forward pass, TEAM-VLA achieves a balanced trade-off between efficiency and effectiveness, without any retraining or parameter updates. Extensive experiments on LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that TEAM-VLA consistently improves inference speed while maintaining or even surpassing the task success rate of full VLA models.

title: Toward Artificial Nociceptors Based on Memristor-Like MEMS Resonator
authors: Erion Uka, Chun Zhao
publish: Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems
abstract: For the first time, this paper reports the observation of the four key characteristics of a nociceptor, namely the threshold, relaxation, no-adaptation and sensitization characteristics using a generic, silicon-based MEMS resonator. This is achieved by employing a parametric modulation signal to obtain a pinched hysteresis response, a hallmark for memristor-like behavior, enabling the realization of a proof-of-concept resonant MEMS artificial nociceptor. The proposed approach shows significant promise towards an easy-to-fabricate, silicon-based resonant MEMS neuromorphic sensor with integrated nociceptor functionality (for in-sensor/in-MEMS computing). This is ideal for applications requiring low-cost, small-footprint, and integrated sense-and-compute functionality, for example, humanoid robotics, and Internet-of-Things capable devices for in-sensor/edge computing. The presented work also shows promise as a highly specific switch/trigger, which could be useful for several applications, such as healthcare and environmental monitoring.[2025-0238]

title: PLL-Synchronized PWM Rectifier: Cascaded In-Loop Low-Pass Filtering Approaches
authors: Hafiz Ahmed, Adel Rahoui, Seifeddine Ben Elghali
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: Amplitude normalization schemes (ANSs) are essential for making PLL dynamics independent of grid amplitude - a critical feature for the stable control of grid-connected power converters. This article presents a comparative analysis of recently proposed low-pass filter (LPF)-based ANS-PLLs and introduces an improved demodulation-based PLL (dPLL) featuring an LPF in the feedforward path. Through small-signal modeling and the symmetric optimum method, we establish that while these architectures share similar open-loop characteristics, existing PLLs require a significantly higher LPF cut-off frequency to achieve an equivalent phase margin. This necessary compromise inherently degrades frequency tracking and harmonic robustness. The proposed dPLL simplifies implementation with one fewer tuning gain while maintaining real-time compatible computational complexity. Additionally, numerical phase-plane analysis demonstrates the global convergence of the dPLL under a wide initial phase estimation error range <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(-\pi,\pi)$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Experimental validation on an AC-DC PWM rectifier confirms the superior performance of the proposed method in reducing source current harmonic distortion across diverse operating conditions.

title: Critical Success Factors for Sustainable Village Tourism Development: A Conceptual Framework
authors: Wahyudhi Sutrisno, Elisa Kusrini, Winda Nur Cahyo, Agus Mansur
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Village tourism has increasingly been recognized as a strategic approach to achieving sustainable development through community participation, cultural preservation, and local economic growth. However, its rapid expansion is often not accompanied by integrated and systematically structured frameworks that support effective management and long-term sustainability. This study aims to identify Critical Success Factors (CSFs) that underpin sustainable village tourism development and to synthesize them into a coherent conceptual framework. A Systematic Literature Review (SLR) was conducted using peer-reviewed journal articles published between 2020 and 2025, and thematic analysis was employed to extract recurring and robust success factors across diverse empirical contexts. The findings identify six core CSFs: infrastructure and accessibility, authentic and socially embedded tourism experiences, digital marketing and technological innovation, environmental sustainability and social responsibility, managerial capacity and local leadership, and destination reputation grounded in local cultural identity. The study advances the literature by proposing an integrated and systembased conceptual framework that captures the interdependent relationships among CSFs. The framework provides a structured foundation for future empirical research and offers strategic insights to support more coherent and sustainable village tourism management.

title: Simulation Metal Particle Motion Characteristics Particle Tracking-Multibody Dynamics GIS
authors: Chen Cao, Xu Yang, HaoZhen Li, Ruifeng Hua, Xingwei Wang, Xichi Zhu, Hewei Huang
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: The generation of metallic particles is an inevitable byproduct during the manufacturing, installation, and operation of Gas Insulated Switchgear (GIS). Under the influence of electric field forces, these micro-particles undergo saltation, which can significantly compromise the system's insulation performance and, in severe cases, trigger major system accidents such as equipment burnout. Consequently, characterizing the kinematic properties of these metallic particles presents a formidable challenge. While conventional approaches predominantly focus on simplified kinematic trajectories, this study builds upon them by developing a comprehensive three-dimensional (3D) simulation model of particle motion. This is achieved through the coupling of electric fields, particle tracking, multibody dynamics (MBD), and acoustic fields, utilizing fluid-flow-based particle tracking simulations. Furthermore, an MBD-driven vibration signal simulation model is established to capture the transient signals generated during particle collisions, effectively addressing the limitations of direct internal observation for metallic particle defects within the GIS cavity. The results indicate that under power-frequency AC excitation, the motion of spherical metallic particles predominantly manifests as frequent collisions with the inner surface of the grounded enclosure. The maximum velocity of the particles during collision reaches <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.318 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>. Based on the developed GIS vibration signal simulation model, by solving for the collision force between the metallic particle and the enclosure, a vibration acceleration of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.0032 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$</tex> is obtained under power-frequency AC excitation. This quantitative parameter provides a critical diagnostic benchmark for identifying internal metallic particle defects in GIS.

title: An Intelligent Ensemble Learning Framework for Diabetes Risk Prediction with Gender- and Age-Aware Analytics
authors: K. Ananthi, J.S. Devaprasad, M. NitheesRaj, C. Vijay, K. Vishnu
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Early diagnosis of diabetes is important in preventive medicine and the successful treatment of the illness. The more advanced model of diabetes prediction that combines several methods of ensemble learning to reach high accuracy, stability, and time-sensitive health evaluation. The Ensemble Learning Technique combines several classifiers and leverages their complementary strengths to improve predictive performance and minimize overfitting. Massive data preprocessing is conducted to fill missing values, normalize attributes, and eliminate noise, and feature selection is performed to preserve the most useful features and maximize model efficiency. The demographic stratification of the population, based on age and gender, as well as clinical features, is also proposed in the approach and will ultimately permit determining the population's risk patterns. Such segmentation can help better understand the role of demographic variables in diabetes risk and design tailor-made prediction and intervention programs. The proposed ensemble-based framework is also experimentally tested in a design that is conscious of demographics. The model predicts at least 90 % and averages 0.92 across repeat validation experiments, with a standard deviation of 0.03. The demographic sensitivity is further <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.93-0.02$</tex>, which is strong to changes in age and gender. The paper presents the potential of ensemble machine learning in conjunction with demographic analysis to enhance predictive healthcare and support data-driven decision-making in public health.

title: Intelligent Structural and Motion Analysis of an Omnidirectional Four-Wheel Mobile Robot for Smart Automation
authors: A P Mohan Raj, Parameshwaran P, Sanjay Karthi T, Sudharsan K, Aadhavan K
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The four-wheel mobile robot is omnidirectional and allows motion on planes in all directions without changing the angle of steering the wheels, which offers high manoeuvrability in smart automation and constrained spaces. In this paper, an intelligent framework of structure and motion analysis is suggested where one corner of the chassis is used as the forward reference, and movement can be made in eight directions. As opposed to the traditional square chassis designs with fixed ninety-degree distance between the wheels, the proposed design systematically alters the front and rear wheel mounting angles to explore their effects on both mobility and stability. An embedded microcontroller is used to provide fine speed control and synchronized motion of each wheel by a 12 V DC motor and control. An elaborate mathematical model of the kinematics of wheels and rollers is drawn up to determine a correlation between wheel orientation, velocity components, and consequent robot motion. To test the machinability of the wheels, the accuracy of the trajectory tracking, direction stability, and load distribution of various wheel angle configurations are experimentally validated on a smooth cement surface. The outcomes demonstrate that the change in the wheel angle would influence the motion efficiency, precision, and final performance significantly. A range of wheel angles is identified that is optimum in terms of improving omnidirectional capability, and the control complexity is not high. The research offers useful design principles to enhance chassis design for flexible automation and mobile robotic platform design.

title: Adaptive Gated Hybrid CNN Swin Transformers for Stage Aware Sugarcane Anthracnose Diagnosis
authors: T V Prathima, S. Kaliappan, R Sujitha, Ritika Joshi
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Anthracnose of the sugarcane leaf by Colletotrichum species is a high-impact foliar pathogen necessitating stage-sensitive intervention to avoid the systemic loss of crops. Automation makes diagnosis more complex due to the smooth changes of lesions and the interference of the environmental background. The current paper presents the Hybrid CNN-Swin Transformer (HCST) architecture that combines hierarchical self-attention with convolutional inductive biases. The model makes use of a CNN stem to encode local necrotic textures and a Swin Transformer backbone in order to enable global spatial dependence capture through shifted-window self-attention. It uses a novel Adaptive Gated Fusion mechanism to balance between local and global embeddings dynamically so as to maximize the distinction between Healthy and Early stages, as well as between Moderate and Severe ones. HCST would perform at a state-of-the-art of 96.8, 96.2, and 0.992 Accuracy, Macro-F1, and macro-AUROC, respectively, on a field-captured dataset of 6,400 images. Particularly, the model minimized the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) to 0.018, which showed that the model was highly reliable in terms of its probabilistic capability of being deployed in the field. The framework will exhibit better resilience to field scale variability, offering a large-scale approach to early disease control and precise cultivation of sugarcane.

title: Design and Application of a Self-Aligning Control Circuit Using Operational Amplifier Configurations
authors: Karl Fredrich C. Watiwat, Char Chaela C. Manzano, Ken Kirby A. Pabito, Anne Margarette Z. Sagun, Mariz J. Prado, Mirasol C. Dilay, Anela L. Salvador, Selverino A. Magon
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: This project presents a versatile analog control architecture combining self-alignment, adaptive regulation, and supervisory decision-making. The system features a dual-loop feedback mechanism with a differential sensor alignment loop, processvariable synthesis, a windowed supervisory comparator, and an automatic gain control (AGC) stage. A light-responsive implementation demonstrates automatic sensor alignment, stable output regulation, and seamless switching between proportional and regulated modes. Simulation results confirm rapid convergence, well-damped transient response, and robust, bounded operation under varying inputs. Beyond illumination control, the architecture is applicable to industrial automation, temperature and fluid-level regulation, environmental monitoring, and precision instrumentation. By leveraging continuoustime analog loops, the design achieves low-latency, deterministic, and energy-efficient control without digital processing. This work highlights the advantages of modular analog control systems for practical applications and as a transparent platform for illustrating classical control principles.

title: 3D Scene Reconstruction From 2D Images for Smart Assistance and Interaction
authors: Shivika Mittal, Manya Saini, Misha Kakkar
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Economical optical sensors capable of accurately interpret scenes in three dimensions have become essential for indoor smart assistance systems, including assistive technologies for elderly care, intelligent home monitoring, and domestic service robots. We offer an artificial intelligence design for this technology which employs 2D pictures for creating complex models in 3D. This technique merges conventional three-dimensional geometry methodologies alongside deep learning-powered distance calculation. The whole procedure, consisting of detecting objects, height determination, point network formation, ground reconfiguration, and security-focused proximity calculation, has been constructed to operate well on standard hardware used in computers. The framework produces exact distance projections, produces accurate 3dimensional networks, and retains an accuracy that suits active situations in real time, in line with tests done interior.

title: Development and Validation of an LQR-Optimized State-Feedback Controller for DC Motor Position Control
authors: Anthony Abesado, Christian Arnel Alcantara, Edward Panganiban
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: This study presents the design, simulation, and performance evaluation of an optimal state-feedback controller for precise position regulation of a permanent-magnet DC motor. A detailed electromechanical model of the motor is formulated in state-space form to accurately represent both electrical and mechanical dynamics. A Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) is then designed to minimize a quadratic cost function that balances tracking accuracy and control effort. Systematic tuning of the LQR weighting matrices ensures that key performance specifications are satisfied, including a settling time below 1.5 seconds, overshoot under 5%, and negligible steady-state error, while maintaining control effort within practical limits. The controller's effectiveness is verified through MATLAB/Simulink simulations, where step reference tracking, disturbance rejection, and robustness against parameter variations are examined. Simulation results confirm that the LQR-based controller delivers fast, stable, and accurate position control with smooth control actions and strong disturbance rejection. These findings demonstrate the LQR controller's suitability for highperformance DC motor positioning applications, offering a scalable solution for both academic and industrial systems requiring precise motion control.

title: An Inverse Problem for Cellular Automata on Probability Measures with Application to Pollution Diffusion Modelling
authors: Amelia Kunze, Enrico Formenti, Davide La Torre
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Classical Cellular Automata (CA) are a powerful computational framework for modeling global spatio-temporal dynamics with local interactions. While CA have been applied across numerous scientific fields, identifying the local rule that governs observed dynamics remains a challenging task. Moreover, the underlying assumption of deterministic cell states often limits the applicability of CA to systems characterized by inherent uncertainty. To address this limitation, we have recently proposed Cellular Automata on Probability Measures (CAMs) which replace the classical state set with a probability measure over the set of all possible states. In this paper, we propose an inverse problem for our CAM model. We formalize the search for the local rule of an unknown CAM as a constrained optimization problem. Using a genetic algorithm, we illustrate how solving our inverse problem yields a CAM which describes the dynamics of real-world pollution diffusion.

title: Leaf Disease Detection using Classical Image Processing and Rule-based Analysis
authors: Sumit Dubey, Khushal Patel, Taizeeba Walele, Chintal Gala, Swati Nadkarni, Vinit Kotak
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Early detection of leaf diseases is critical to minimize crop losses, improve food security, and promote sustainable agricultural practices. While recent approaches based on deep learning have demonstrated high accuracy, they typically require large datasets, high computational resources, and reliable internet connectivity, limiting their usability in rural and resource-constrained environments. This paper presents a lightweight, Python-based leaf disease detection system that leverages classical image processing techniques and is deployed as a PC-based web application. The proposed solution is specifically designed for offline operation on basic laptop or desktop systems, enabling farmers and agricultural practitioners to upload leaf images, obtain disease classification, visualize infected regions, and receive appropriate treatment recommendations. Experimental results demonstrate that the system provides an interpretable, cost-effective, and scalable alternative to complex models, effectively bridging the gap between research innovations and real-world agricultural deployment in low-resource settings.

title: Decentralized Blockchain Framework for Tamper-Proof Cloud Data Storage and Retrieval
authors: Prathusha Perugu, Thummala Varshini, Anumula Akhil Sai, Eeranolla Yojitha, Thangillapalli Venkata Chaithanya
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: Cloud computing became one of the most important technologies to avail comfortable storage facility, it really fulfilling today’s industry needs. This wide usage is also causing problems like privacy, integrity, and trust. Cloud computing technology in general depends on centralized storage, this model is suffering with the said problems. To overcome that issues present came with decentralised blockchain technology for tamper-proof cloud data storage and Retrieval (DBF-TCDSR) to maintain the transparency, security and verifiable data management. In this model when the user uploads the data the mechanisms splits into multiple parts and stores in multiple decentralized nodes to avoid single point failures., the system maintains the transactions log data, storage proofs, and most importantly maintains immutable metadata, and smart contracts automatically verifies access control and reward mechanisms. AI technology also using to identify reliable nodes for storage based on their historical behaviour and latency. A Proof of Resource Reputation (PoRR) agreement mechanism using for maintaining fair participation and energy-efficient validation. In testing proves that DBF-TCDSR provides better result in reducing retrieval latency and verification overhead while improving data integrity and confidentiality. A tamper-proof foundation for next-generation security in cloud storage environment.

title: TCAD Simulation and Analytical Investigation of a Lateral Tunnel FET Biosensor for Biomolecule Detection
authors: N. Pathmavathi, I. Vivek Anand, S.Mathu Suthan, M. Manoj Kumar
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The primary goal of modern semiconductor technology is to lower the power consumption of semiconductor devices while increasing their speed. The size of transistors is aggressively scaled to keep up with Moore's law, and the continual shrinkage has placed the Boltzmann barrier (60mV/decade) on the transistors due to fundamental physics. The scaling of traditional MOSFETs has prompted research into developing technologies that have lower power requirements, lower short channel effects (SCEs), and steeper sub-threshold slopesThe subthreshold swing is the inverse of the change in current which can be produced as a unit change in gate voltage. This is a critical factor limiting the gate voltage. To evade all the limitations, there are several multi-gate architectures suggested. The tunnel field-effect transistor is considered to be one of the good candidates in low-power applications among field-effect transistors. Tunnel Field-Effect Transistor (TFET) is a reverse-bias device that is described to have a p-type/intrinsic/n-type structure, that is, majority carriers are injected in the source to the channel by means of Band-to-Band Tunneling (BTBT). The TFET, however, has a disadvantage of low ON current and this in turn reduces the overall performance of the device. This drawback causes ambipolar behaviour to increase, which is manifested in leakage current. Enhancing the ON current requires a number of lateral heterostructures have been introduced where the source energy bandgap is set to be lesser than that of the channel. With this bandgap setup, there is an enhancement of the tunnelling probability, and hence, the drain current is enhanced. The objective of this work is to develop an analytical model for lateral TFETs incorporating the biosensor.

title: A Case-Density-Driven Closed-Loop Intelligent Strategy for Air-Ground-Human Collaborative Disinfection: An End-To-End Framework from Case Perception to Task Scheduling
authors: Liuhua Zhang, Xin Liao, Zhengquan Li, Nanfeng Zhang, Jingfeng Yang, Yingyi Wu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: Traditional public health disinfection strategies rely on static area coverage and pre-defined scheduling, leading to inefficient resource utilization and delayed responses under dynamic epidemic conditions. This paper proposes a case-densitydriven closed-loop intelligent strategy for air-ground-human (AGH) collaborative disinfection. A dynamic spatiotemporal risk field is constructed using case density and population mobility data, enabling adaptive prioritization of high-risk regions. A multi-objective scheduling model jointly optimizes coverage rate, suppression timeliness, operational efficiency, resource utilization, and system robustness. A rolling closed-loop replanning mechanism integrates real-time execution feedback and disturbance adaptation. Experimental results across representative urban scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method improves coverage by approximately 10%, reduces time-to-control by nearly 50%, and enhances robustness under disturbances compared with static and traditional multi-objective methods. The framework provides an efficient and resilient solution for intelligent epidemic-response disinfection.

title: Sampling Strategy Design for Model Predictive Path Integral Control on Legged Robot Locomotion
authors: Chuyuan Tao, Fanxin Wang, Haolong Jiang, Jia He, Yiyang Chen, Qinglei Bu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Control and Robotics (EECR)
abstract: Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control has emerged as a powerful sampling-based optimal control method for complex, nonlinear, and high-dimensional systems. However, directly applying MPPI to legged robotic systems presents several challenges. This paper systematically investigates the role of sampling strategy design within the MPPI framework for legged robot locomotion. Based upon the idea of structured control parameterization, we explore and compare multiple sampling strategies within the framework, including both unstructured and spline-based approaches. Through extensive simulations on a quadruped robot platform, we evaluate how different sampling strategies affect control smoothness, task performance, robustness, and sample efficiency. The results provide new insights into the practical implications of sampling design for deploying MPPI on complex legged systems.

title: A Reusable Sample Feeding and Sealing Mechanism for Lunar Regolith In-situ Analysis
authors: Yafang Liu, Junyue Tang, Tianqi Wang, Yinchao Wang, Honglun Yang, Shengyuan Jiang
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: The Chang’e-7 mission will achieve a landing on the lunar South Pole to explore the surface characteristics, internal properties, and space environment of the lunar South Pole region. The Lunar Soil Volatile Analyzer is mounted on the rover. It uses the robotic arm’s digging tool to establish lunar soil profiles at different depths on the lunar surface. After quantitatively collecting lunar soil in-situ, the analyzer transfers the soil to an induction heating furnace for heating. The released volatiles are continuously measured at multiple temperature points to obtain the volatile compositions derived from lunar soil adsorption, solar wind implantation, cosmic ray causes, and radioactive causes. This provides a scientific basis for exploring the source of nitrogen on the lunar surface, regional geological chronology, and lunar soil maturity. This paper mainly introduces the processes of the lunar soil volatile preparation unit, as well as the main structural designs and working principles involved in the key preparation units. The processes mainly include sample receiving, sample transfer, sample preparation, and sample discarding. In the introduction of the process flow, the design of key components in each process and the results of characteristic test verification for these components will be emphasized, providing theoretical support for the preparation and measurement of volatiles.

title: Information-and-Measurement Model for Validating Engineering Operating Procedures of Unmanned Systems Using Simulator Event Data
authors: E. S. Kostin, A. S. Kostin
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This paper proposes a method for the formalized validation of engineering actions performed by a maintainer/operator of unmanned systems in a virtual training simulator treated as an information-and-measurement system. The simulator generates a dynamic event log that records process parameters (tool, target object, operating modes, measured quantities). A reference procedure is specified by a parameterized finite-state machine with guard conditions that capture technological tolerances and safety requirements. An algorithm for matching the event log to the reference scenario, a taxonomy of procedural and parametric violations, and a mechanism for generating an interpretable validation report are developed. The approach is applicable to training tasks involving assembly, soldering, and diagnostics of unmanned-system subsystems.

title: A Robust Hybrid Solar–Wind–Battery based Standalone DC Microgrid for Reliable Off-Grid Power Supply
authors: K.Divya, Deshath M, Karthikesan P, Srinath S, Yuvaraj M
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The increasing demand for reliable electricity in remote and off-grid regions has accelerated the adoption of decentralized renewable energy systems. This paper presents the design, development, and experimental realization of a hybrid standalone DC microgrid integrating a Solar Photovoltaic (PV) system, a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG)–based Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS), and a battery energy storage unit. The proposed architecture is entirely hardware-oriented and focuses on real-time power generation, regulation, and energy management without reliance on simulation-based validation. Solar and wind sources are interfaced to a common DC bus through dedicated power electronic converters, enabling effective voltage regulation and maximum power extraction. A battery storage system is incorporated via a bidirectional DC–DC converter to maintain DC bus stability during source intermittency and load fluctuations. An embedded controller executes energy management logic to coordinate power sharing among the sources and storage system. Experimental observations demonstrate stable DC bus operation, effective utilization of renewable resources, and uninterrupted power supply under varying operating conditions. The proposed standalone DC microgrid offers a practical, scalable, and environmentally sustainable solution for rural electrification, telecommunication infrastructure, and isolated industrial loads.

title: An Integrated Explainable AI Architecture with Hybrid Attribution and Fairness Monitoring for Machine Learning
authors: Shakthi Kalyan S, Jamuna R
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The growing use of machine learning models in areas of high stakes like healthcare, finance, and governance has raised the issue on the use of transparent and interpretable decision-making systems. Conventional artificial intelligence systems tend to be black-box systems and as such their stakeholders may not be able to interpret the thought processes behind forecasting. In order to handle this issue, this project recommends XplainAI, a unified explainable artificial intelligence system that is capable of instilling interpretability in the machine learning life cycle. The system is a combination of several explanation methods to produce both global and local explanations of predictive model, such as SHAP, LIME, and gradient-based attribution methods. The hybrid explanation mechanism is presented to enhance the stability and fidelity of the interpretation results besides monitoring the importance of features in the training process. The Adult Census Income dataset was used to perform experiments to determine the performance of classifications and reliability of explanations. The proposed framework had a prediction accuracy of 96.41 which was better than traditional models like Random Forest, Gradient Boosting and Support Vector Machines. There is also a reliable interpretability with explanation fidelity and stability of 0.94 and 0.91, respectively. The findings substantiate the fact that the explicit inclusion of explainability in a machine learning pipeline enhances the levels of transparency and predictability by intelligent decision-support systems.

title: IoT-Enabled Intelligent Smart Fitness Bands for Muscle Growth and Performance Analytics Using EMG Signals
authors: Deeparani. M, Bhuvaneshwaran. B, Abishek. N, Sangeethraj. B, Ashokkumar. K
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: An Internet of Things-enabled intelligent smart fitness band for muscle growth assessment and performance analytics through electromyography signal monitoring. Conventional approaches for fitness training, rehabilitation, and clinical assessment depend on manual muscle testing or wired electromyography systems, which limit portability, continuous observation, and remote supervision. The proposed wearable system integrates surface electromyography sensors within a compact fitness band to capture real-time muscle activity during physical exercise. A NodeMCU ESP8266 microcontroller manages data acquisition and transmits processed signals to a cloud-based monitoring environment using the Thing Speak platform, enabling secure storage, visualization, and long-term performance analysis. Machine intelligence-driven analytics support training feedback, fatigue identification, and muscle growth trend evaluation. Experimental validation demonstrates an accuracy of 88% when compared with conventional reference systems. The wireless and wearable design improves accessibility, responsiveness, and user engagement through web and mobile interfaces. The results indicate strong potential for applications in sport science, physiotherapy, injury prevention, and personalized fitness training. Integration of wearable electromyography sensing with cloud analytics and intelligent feedback establishes a scalable framework for next-generation smart fitness and rehabilitation solutions. The proposed method achieved 88% signal accuracy, one to two second latency, portability score 9, full remote accessibility, automated fatigue detection, and real time cloud based muscle monitoring performance. The IoT-based EMG smart band achieved 88% signal accuracy, 1-2 s transmission latency, and 500-1000 Hz sampling frequency. Portability scored 9 / 10, with 100% remote accessibility via cloud platforms.

title: Queueing-Theoretic and Reinforcement Learning-Based Admission Control for 5G/6G Network Slicing: Synthetic Traffic Models
authors: E Sindhu, T. Roseline Velankanni, Diya Agnihotri, Md Ankushavali
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Admission control is one of the most important enablers of 5G/6G network slicing because heterogeneous slice requests compete for the limited shared resources, and should be accepted or rejected at arrival time, subject to service level constraints. This paper models a three-class slicing system (eMBB, URLLC, and mMTC) with Poisson arrivals and exponential holding time and formulates online admission as a Markov decision process, observed at request arrival instants. A lightweight tabular Q-learning controller is proposed that learns long-term revenue-aware admission decisions from unlabeled traffic traces without requiring an explicit traffic predictor. Using a controlled simulation study with an admit-if-feasible greedy baseline, acceptance, blocking, and profit are evaluated across a range of offered loads. Results show that the learned policy preserves acceptance performance while improving provider profit under saturation; for example, profit increases by 1.06% at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda=0.31$</tex> and by 1.96% at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda=0.50$</tex> with comparable blocking. The approach offers interpretable decision logic and low per-arrival complexity, supporting deployment-oriented slice admission in resource-constrained cores.

title: Implementation and Performance Analysis of Proposed ANN model for Unreinforced Exterior RC Beam-Column Joint Strength
authors: Mohamed Salama, Ranim Maatouq
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: Shear strength of unreinforced joint in reinforced concrete building is very crucial to be calculated for structural safety. Old buildings followed old codes of design and were built with little or no transverse reinforcement in the joint of the column and beam connection. The prediction of such joint strength is predicted by using artificial neural network for capturing the nonlinear relationship between the inputs that affect the joint strength and the output. The proposed model (ANN-7-13-1) showed high performance in the prediction of unreinforced joint shear strength.

title: Real-Time Offensive Speech Detection in Google Meet using Speech-to-Text and NLP
authors: Gopinath S, Sheeja Sudheer
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: This paper offers a real-time speech-based attack detection system in an online meeting platform like Google meet with a combination of Speech-to-Text (STT) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) model. The suggested system captures live audio streams and transcribes speech with an offline STT engine and classifies the obtained text with a fine refined DistilBERT model. The system is also locally executed unlike the current systems, which use a cloud-based processing mechanism, which guarantees local privacy of users and low latency. Constituent datasets such as OLID and HateXplain are used to train the model, making it possible to effectively identify explicit and contextual offensive words. Experimental findings indicate that the proposed system has an accuracy of 95.6% with an average processing latency of below 2 seconds per audio segment. Also, the system uses real time alert generation and logging applications to enhance accountability without affecting the current communication. The modular architecture allows scalability and adaptability to other video conferencing platforms. Generally, the solution suggested is an effective, non-invasive and scalable method of off-putting speech moderation in cyberspace communication settings.

title: Intelligent Multi-Agent Reinforcement Sanitizer for Securing LLM Systems
authors: Pramila P, Nadana Ravishankar T
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: LMs are increasingly vulnerable to prompt poisoning, where adversarial instructions cause the model to behave unexpectedly. Current defense mechanisms have been inadequate against attacks that are paraphrased or in another language, as well as against multimodal or multi-turn attacks, and generally lack reliable mechanisms for protecting hidden prompts from the system. This paper describes IMARS, a new multi-agent defense pipeline designed to help defend against prompt poisoning of large language models (LLMs). IMARS integrates multiple elements, including semantic contrastive detection, attention-based anomaly scoring, cryptographic integrity verification, iterative sanitization, temporal analysis, and explainability, to form a new pipeline to protect LLMs. IMARS integrates text and OCR inputs to provide multimodal data for the inspection, while also using drift-aware contextual reasoning to detect changes in attack patterns over time. The goal of IMARS is to create a reliable, near real-time, and interpretable security framework that improves LLM safety against sophisticated prompt poisoning attacks while allowing for transparency in the decision-making process. Experimental validation of IMARS effectiveness in the detection and mitigation of sophisticated prompt injection attempts has shown success across a variety of attack conditions.

title: Developing Machine Learning Models for Automated Text Classification in Legal Documents: A Traditional NLP Approach
authors: Mr. Suraj Gupta, Natthan Singh, Er. Deepali Avasthi, Er. Chandan Mani Tripathi
publish: 2026 ASU International Conference in Emerging Technologies for Sustainability and Intelligent Systems (ICETSIS)
abstract: The growing volume in legal documentation presents a substantial challenge to the efficiency of the judicial system, particularly in socially critical areas such as cases related to dowry and domestic violence. Automated text classification systems are essential to simplify case management and support judicial research in different regions or states. This paper proposes an approach for the automated classification of legal judgments related to dowry cases under relevant sections of the Indian Penal Code (IPC). Unlike studies relying on publicly available datasets, this research mainly focuses on expert-classified dataset of judgmental documents manually collected from district courts in Azamgarh, Ballia and Ghazipur city providing a geographically and judicially specific corpus focused on judgmental outcomes. We employ Natural Language Processing (NLP) Protocols, specifically Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF), for feature vectorization, paired with classical Machine Learning architectures to perform supervised classification. Our methodology successfully transforms the unstructured judicial text into a vector space model which is suitable for prediction, achieving an high Accuracy, Precision Metric, F1-score Metric and Recall Metric across the defined categories. The Results demonstrate the efficacy of combining TF-IDF and classical ML approaches for accurate domain-specific classification, offering a scalable model that can significantly assist legal professionals in classifying cases and conducting targeted judicial analysis within the Indian legal context.

title: Calculation of a Cooled Compressor Accounting for Friction
authors: E.V. Dzhuraeva, V. S. Okhotin
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Technologies in Engineering Education (Inforino)
abstract: The article examines the compression process in a cooled compressor, taking into account irreversible friction losses that have a significant impact on its efficiency. Based on the first law of thermodynamics, equations for a nonequilibrium process are obtained, which can be used for a qualitative analysis of the process. The impact of frictional losses on the polytropic index and compressor efficiency (COP) was analyzed. Calculations were performed for an air compressor with friction and cooled, using the following inlet conditions: temperature of 20 ℃, and pressure of 0,1 MPa, and pressure rations of 6, 8, 10 and 12. The minimum possible value of isothermal efficiency were obtained, the limit values of the actual polytropic index were determined, and the value of friction heat were calculated. The polytropic index due to friction – generated heat being absorbed by the gas. The article present tables and graphs showing the dependence of frictional heat on isothermal efficiency and the pressure ratio in the compressor.

title: Investigation of the Optimal Number of Packets per Acknowledgment for the QUIC Protocol in Wireless Networks
authors: A. M. Turlikov, E. S. Markovskiy
publish: 2026 Wave Electronics and its Application in Information and Telecommunication Systems (WECONF)
abstract: This paper examines the fundamental principles of the QUIC protocol, focusing on the parameter determining the number of packets acknowledged by a single receipt. Since this parameter is not strictly standardized, the aim of the study is to determine its value that maximizes channel utilization. To address this problem, a simplified model of the QUIC protocol was developed, which was used to analyze the optimal receipt size. In accordance with this model, a simulation program was written in Python, allowing the numerical results to be obtained and visualized. This analysis yields conclusions about the dependence of the optimal number of packets in a single receipt on various packet and receipt loss probabilities.

title: Sliding mode control for semi-Markov switching power models with unknown semi-Markov kernel
authors: Liang Chen, Jiao Liu, Junlin Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 11th International Conference on Control Science and Systems Engineering (ICCSSE)
abstract: This study focuses on the sliding mode control method for semi-Markov switching power systems with unknown semi-Markov kernel. In order to meet the complex modeling requirements of the current industrial environment, a semiMarkov switching model is adopted to precisely model the power system. Considering the difficulty in obtaining the relevant properties of semi-Markov kernel in practical applications, this study assumes that partial information of the semi-Markov kernel is unknown. Compared with the semi-Markov jump model with complete semi-Markov kernel information, the conservatism is reduced. Based on the Lyapunov function related to the current system mode and sojourn time, as well as the semi-Markov kernel framework, the mean-square stability conditions of the system are derived. A suitable sliding mode control strategy is designed to overcome the influence of uncertain parameters, and the reachability of the discrete quasi-sliding mode is achieved. Finally, the effectiveness of the sliding mode control strategy designed in this study is verified through numerical simulations.

title: Characterizing Failures of Deep-Learning Models Under Data Paucity for Wireless Time-Varying Channel Estimation
authors: Reihaneh Gh. Roshan, Mohammad Rostami, Atik Faysal, Huaxia Wang, Nikhil Muralidhar
publish: 2026 35th Wireless and Optical Communications Conference (WOCC)
abstract: Fast time-varying propagation environments in 5G/6G wireless systems require channel estimators that can rapidly adapt to unseen channel conditions with very limited labeled data. Conventional pilot-based estimators such as least squares (LS) are robust but inaccurate in low-SNR regimes. Deep learning-based approaches improve reconstruction accuracy but often require retraining across heterogeneous channel statistics, Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) levels, often with a large training data volume. However, most wireless environments incur a high cost during training data collection and hence channel estimation in such scenarios requires models that are effective training under data paucity. Despite its importance, this question has not been investigated thoroughly in wireless channel estimation. To this end, this work presents a rigorous investigation of the performance of deep learning-based channel estimation models, compared to traditional channel estimators, under time-varying channels also suffering from data paucity. We undertake this investigation across multiple line-of-sight (LOS) and non line-of-sight (NLOS) channel models under varying noise conditions. Our findings reveal that deep learning models exhibit significant degradation under data paucity, particularly in noisy, high-dimensional, and distribution-shifted environments, while classical estimators remain robust. These results highlight fundamental limitations of current data-driven approaches for wireless channel estimation under limited data.

title: An Optimized Explainable Transformer-based Intrusion Detection System for Enhancing IoT Security
authors: M. Petchiammal, S. Mary Evanchalin, R. Mallika Pandeeswari, P. J. Beslin Pajila, G. Tamilpavai, R. Ravi
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The increasing number of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies has designed a new dimension of security threats due to the increasing number of interconnected devices. IoT networks are extremely vulnerable to attacks due to their heterogeneous environment, and continuous data transfer, making it a necessity to have efficient intrusion detection systems (IDS) to ensure network security. This research proposes an intelligent intrusion detection framework Explainable Spotted deer transformer system (ESDTS) that aims to improve detection accuracy and interpretability as well as computational complexity in IoT networks. First, the data related to IoT intrusion detection is gathered and preprocessed using various techniques such as data cleaning, encoding, normalization, and noise removal. After that, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) is used to interpret the results of feature importance analysis to provide insights into the important attributes of network traffic. As per the results of importance analysis, the Spotted Deer Optimization algorithm (SDO) is used to select the optimal feature subset to eliminate redundant features and increase the learning process's effectiveness. Finally, the optimal features are used by the Transformer model to detect intrusions. The experimental results show better performance F score, recall, precision, accuracy and error rate compared to the other methods. It confirmed that the proposed framework is capable of achieving a combination between interpretability and detection accuracy.

title: Transformer Augmented Mask R-CNN with Variational Denoising Autoencoder for Robust EEG Signal Reconstruction
authors: M.Rama, T Nalini, Arulanantham G
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Intelligent Communication Technologies and Virtual Mobile Networks (ICICV)
abstract: The numerous overlapping noise sources that may cause electroencephalogram (EEG) recordings include ocular artifacts, electromyographic interference, and powerline contamination. These aberrations have a negative effect on brain-computer interface (BCI) performance, and also diminish the reliability of subsequent neurological analysis to a significant degree. This study proposes the Transformer-Augmented Mask R-CNN with Variational Denoising Autoencoder (TMR-VDAE), a single spatial-temporal probabilistic model of dependable EEG signal regeneration. Although a multi-head Transformer encoder recodes long-range temporal dependencies between signal epochs, an instance-level spatial segmentation is applied to the Mask R-CNN module to recognize and encode areas at risk of artifact occurrence. A variational bottleneck enhances generalization in a range of noisy conditions by modeling probabilistic latent representations. Training is guided by a hybrid multi-objective loss, which includes mask segmentation, loss, KL divergence regularization, and signal reconstruction loss. The experiments on four benchmark datasets (PhysioNet EEG Motor, BCI Competition IV-2a, BCI Competition IV-2b, and SEED) are regularly better than five existing baseline models based on the average SNR gain of 11.43 dB, RMSE of 0.0318, PSNR of 28.94 dB, and Pearson correlation of 0.941. Experiments involving ablation confirm that each architectural component has a different contribution to overall performance.

title: Sustainability via Large Language Model Right-sizing
authors: Jennifer Haase, Finn Klessascheck, Jan Mendling, Sebastian Pokutta
publish: IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Computing
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in organizational workflows, raising concerns about energy use, costs, and data sovereignty. While benchmarks often celebrate cutting-edge models, real-world deployment requires a broader question: when is a smaller, locally deployable model “good enough”? This study evaluates eleven proprietary and open-weight LLMs on ten everyday occupational tasks, including summarizing, scheduling, and drafting emails and proposals. Using a dual-LLM evaluation framework, we automated task execution and standardized assessment across ten criteria of quality, accuracy, and ethical responsibility. Results show GPT-4o delivers top performance but with significantly higher costs and environmental impact. Smaller models such as Gemma-3 and Phi-4 performed strongly on most tasks, supporting their viability where cost-efficiency, local use, or privacy matter. A cluster analysis identified three groups-High, Balanced, and Baseline Performance Models-highlighting trade-offs between quality, control, and sustainability. Task type shaped effectiveness: conceptual tasks were most challenging, while aggregation and transformation tasks yielded better outcomes. We argue for shifting from performance-maximizing benchmarks to task- and context-aware sufficiency assessments that reflect organizational priorities. Our approach offers a replicable method to evaluate AI models from a sustainability perspective and provides practical recommendations for responsible LLM deployment.

title: Breaking Time: A Fully Gaussian Framework for Distributed and Continuous-Time SLAM
authors: Davide Ceriola, Simone Ferrari, Luca Di Giammarino, Leonardo Brizi, Giorgio Grisetti
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Continuous-time SLAM provides a principled framework for fusing heterogeneous sensors while estimating smooth trajectories, and is particularly well-suited for handling heterogeneous, asynchronous sensor streams with non-uniform readout patterns, such as rolling shutter cameras, LiDAR scanners, radar sweeps, or event-based sensors. In this work, we introduce G-solver, a fully Gaussian and distributed framework that combines Gaussian Belief Propagation (GBP) with Gaussian Process (GP) motion priors for continuous-time trajectory estimation. Our GP model provides a probabilistic representation of the trajectory, enabling consistent interpolation and the use of data-driven hyperparameters, while GBP offers a scalable message-passing formulation well-suited for decentralized settings. The resulting solver naturally extends to multi-camera scenarios without specialized synchronization or engineering effort. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and real data, including rolling shutter and distributed multi-camera optimization, demonstrating accurate and stable estimation with runtimes comparable to existing continuous-time methods. An open-source implementation is released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/rvp-group/gsolver</uri>.

title: Position-Consistent Thin-Film Pressure Sensing for Robust Object and Posture Recognition
authors: Hongjie Jiang, Quan Hu, Zhuhui Yin, Senjun Ye, Dongpeng Wang, Haoyu Chang
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Flexible thin-film pressure sensors are widely used in tactile sensing applications, yet their performance is often position-dependent, compromising measurement accuracy and repeatability. In this study, a pressure-concentrating structure was developed to guide loads from different contact points to a fixed sensitive region, significantly improving response consistency and low-force detection. The optimized sensors were integrated into a dexterous-hand system for pressure-based weight estimation, while a MobileNetV1-based vision module was used for camera-based object classification. Additionally, a smart cushion equipped with a 4 × 4 sensor array was designed for sitting-posture recognition, with Gaussian-kernel-based reconstruction enabling continuous pressure mapping. Machine learning models, including SVM and random forest, demonstrated high accuracy in both object and posture recognition tasks. The proposed system reduces position-dependent response variation and improves response consistency within the tested force range, enabling reliable multimodal perception and practical applicability in real-time grasping and posture-monitoring scenarios.

title: SleepXNet: DeepSeek-Guided Hybrid Transformer Model for Clinically Interpretable EEG-Based Sleep Stage Classification
authors: Rakesh Kumar Rai, Dushyant Kumar Singh, Nikhilanand Arya
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Sleep-stage classification using EEG signals is crucial for understanding brain activity during sleep; however, the black-box nature of most deep learning models limits their clinical adoption and practical use. This study presents a novel hybrid framework that enhances both classification accuracy and interpretability. The pipeline begins with Spectrum Attention-Based Bandpass Filtering (SABF), which denoises EEG signals and preserves important spectral features. A conditional GAN is used to address class imbalance and missing data by generating synthetic segments. The feature extraction process uses a dual-path strategy: (i) the Fourier-Temporal Fusion Network (FTFNet) captures time-frequency and inter-channel relationships by utilizing TKABNet and FTLNet, and (ii) parallel CNN streams extract spatially complementary features from both raw and wavelet-transformed EEG. These outputs are fused to form a unified representation that supports rich abstraction. The fused features are passed through a Transformer encoder to model long-range dependencies, a bidirectional GRU (Bi-GRU) for local temporal continuity, and a Graph Attention Network (GAT) to capture physiological transitions. The model feeds the combined output into a fully connected layer that uses softmax to predict the final sleep stage. For interpretability, the DeepSeek module integrates SHAP-based attribution, attention map visualization, and neuro-symbolic reasoning (e.g., spindle <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\rightarrow$</tex-math></inline-formula> N2). DeepSeek also generates clinician-style reports with visual and textual justifications. Evaluations on the SleepEDF-20, CAP, and MASS-SS3 datasets show that the model achieves the highest Accuracy on SleepEDF-20 (88.1%) and CAP (82.0%), while maintaining competitive Accuracy on MASS-SS3 (87.5%), demonstrating strong generalization, transparency, and readiness for clinical deployment.

title: Meta-Fluid Reflector-Enabled Inverse Scattering for Frequency-and-Spatial-Diverse Microwave Imaging
authors: Renhuan Liu, Dingfei Ma, Yuehui Cui, Kin-Fai Tong, Baiyang Liu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter presents a sensing system based on a meta-fluid reflector, which proposes a novel operational paradigm by introducing the principles of Fluid Antenna Systems (FAS) into a reconfigurable metasurface. The core concept lies in rapidly reconfiguring the effective scattering aperture to mimic fluid- like spatial agility, thereby generating massive spatial diversity without altering the underlying hardware. The proposed system integrates inverse scattering theory with a compressive sensing algorithm. By exploiting the programmable and broadband properties of the reflector, a joint frequency-spatial diversity measurement pattern is synthesized from the stochastic radiation fields, serving as the basis for target localization via compressive sensing. Full-wave simulation validates the theoretical accuracy and engineering feasibility of the system. Evaluation in multi-target scattering scenarios demonstrates its high-resolution imaging capability (Structural Similarity Index, SSIM > 0.82), highlighting the considerable potential of this high-performance sensing scheme.

title: A Reconfigurable Metasurface with Broadband and Polarization-Independent Characteristics for Holographic Imaging_supp1-3700630.mp4
authors: Weiwen Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Despite enabling dynamic wavefront manipulation for holo-graphic imaging, many reconfigurable metasurfaces still suffer from narrow bandwidth and limited polarization functionality. In this work, a reconfigurable metasurface with broadband and polarization-insensitive properties is designed to generate distinct field images. The designed rotatable meta-atoms enable both linear and circular polarization control and exhibit broadband performance with a relative bandwidth of 33.3%. A 16×16 metasurface prototype is developed, integrating a camera, artificial intelligence computing board, control circuit, motors, and meta-atoms. The device is capable of autonomously executing a series of operations, including metasurface image acquisition and segmentation, angular recognition of individual units, and motorized rotation control. A target structural layout of the metasurface can be realized within approximately 1 second. Strong agreement among theoretical, numerical, and experimental results validates the proposed design. This work innovatively combines reconfigurability, broadband performance, and polarization insensitivity within a unified metasurface platform. Moreover, its mechanical architecture ensures stable functionality even under power-off conditions.

title: Parallel-Elastic Actuation with Reactive Latch Elevates Robotic Hopping Performance: Jump Height and Continuity_supp1-3701316.mp4
authors: Pakpong Chirarattananon
publish: N/A
abstract: While many animals exhibit impressive hopping capabilities, machines have struggled to match their performance. Current hopping robots face limitations in power density, energy efficiency, and control stability. Here, we present a parallel-elastic actuation mechanism with a reactive latch that optimizes energy transfer, enabling a legged robot to achieve hopping heights and continuity previously unattainable. This mechanism efficiently stores and releases energy, extending the actuation period over the aerial phase while minimizing stance time. Our robot achieves a maximum hopping height of 3.6 meters, surpassing both human and animal records while demonstrating sustained, high-frequency hopping cycles with minimal power requirement. By integrating inertia-based onboard sensorimotor autonomy, we demonstrate stable, controlled hopping in environments without external aid. These results represent a step toward bridging the performance gap between biological and robotic locomotion, with potential to influence the design of future legged systems.

title: Trajectory Tracking Control of AUV Based on Adaptive Robust NMPC Controller With Disturbance Compensation
authors: Peng Qian, Zhiyan Li, Zhiwei Sun, Bohan Wang, Xuanang Song, Dahai Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes a novel composite antidisturbance controller aimed at enhancing the trajectory tracking performance of autonomous underwater vehicles under model parameter uncertainties and complex external disturbances. The controller combines robust nonlinear model predictive control (RNMPC) with an L1 adaptive controller to effectively improve the accuracy of the trajectory tracking under external disturbances. The L1 adaptive controller provides real-time disturbance estimation and compensation. Specifically, an adjustable proportional parameter is introduced to reduce the impact of model parameter errors on disturbance observation accuracy. Compared to traditional RNMPC, the proposed control scheme relies less on precise modeling and exhibits an enhanced system robustness against external disturbances alongside improved control accuracy. Numerical simulations validate the superior ability of the controller to reject sudden large disturbances and high-frequency disturbances, even when hydrodynamic parameters contain an average error of 30%. Finally, physical trajectory tracking experiments were conducted under various complex disturbance scenarios which were generated using external thrusters. The results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm improves tracking accuracy by approximately 27% on average.

title: Resonant Cavity Theory and Its Applications Into Common-Mode Resonance Analysis and Suppression in Ultrawideband Phased Arrays
authors: Hao Cheng, De-Ming Sun, Gaobiao Xiao, Xue-Xia Yang, Zhengpeng Wang, Steven Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: The common-mode (CM) resonance is a critical issue in ultrawideband (UWB) tightly coupled phased array systems. Traditional methods of CM signal analysis using circuit theories are not suitable here due to the distinctive characteristic of CM resonance. Thus, currently there is a lack of comprehensive theory for this issue, leading to inaccurate analysis and complex suppression strategies. To address this, this article proposes, for the first time, a resonant cavity theory to rigorously model and analyze the CM resonance in UWB tightly coupled arrays (TCAs). The proposed resonant cavity is constructed from an equivalent rectangular coaxial waveguide with hybrid boundaries of perfect electric conductor (PEC) and perfect magnetic conductor (PMC). To analyze the resonant cavity, decomposition of longitudinal field is performed, where field components are expanded into infinite Fourier series. Then boundary conditions are applied to derive discrete cavity resonant modes. It is demonstrated that CM resonance in UWB TCAs is equivalent to the dominant <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\text {TM}}_{111}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> resonant mode of the proposed resonant cavity. Based on this resonant cavity theory, two practical applications are illustrated. First, a method of accurately predicting CM resonant frequency is developed based on the equivalent circuit model of the cavity, realizing a prediction error below 1.14%. Second, a simple and effective strategy of suppressing CM resonance using CM inductors is introduced. Even- and odd-mode circuit analyses based on the resonant cavity theory are proposed to explain the suppression mechanism. To validate the proposed theory and suppression strategy, one <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\times 6$</tex-math> </inline-formula> UWB TCA is designed and fabricated, which achieves effective CM suppression over 0.86–8.28 GHz (9.63:1) with wide-range scanning ability.

title: A Voltage Correction Virtual Admittance Control of STATCOM for Suppressing SSO in Wind Farms Under Weak Grid
authors: Neng Peng, Jianjun Sun, YangDou Liang, Zhencheng Liang, Xiaoming Zha
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Sub-supersynchronous oscillation (SSO) often arises due to impedance interactions between wind farms (WFs) and weak grid, posing a significant threat to system stability. This article employs a sequence impedance model of a WF integrated with a static synchronous compensator (STATCOM) to investigate the mechanisms and characteristics of SSO under weak-grid conditions. The analysis reveals that reduced grid stiffness leads to a deterioration of system stability. To address this issue, a novel voltage correction virtual admittance (VCVA) control of STATCOM is proposed. The proposed scheme approximately introduces an equivalent passive admittance to reshape the sub-supersynchronous impedance of the WF, thereby providing sufficient damping for SSO suppression. Furthermore, a filter-free adaptive oscillation voltage extraction method based on constant voltage magnitude is developed to support the proposed VCVA control. The inherent DC offset between the extracted and actual oscillation voltage is automatically compensated by the STATCOM DC voltage control. Finally, both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed VCVA control provides enhanced damping performance and faster dynamic response compared with conventional filter-based damping schemes, validating its effectiveness in mitigating SSO in WFs connected to weak grid.
